This document plans a tool that scaffolds the agent workflow (front-load context -> structured plan -> iterative and adversarial review -> isolated implementation -> adversarial review) into a project, so the structure does not have to be hand-rolled each time. It follows the same planning format the tool is meant to scaffold.

## Motivations

- Setting up the workflow by hand for each project is repetitive: the same planning-document skeleton, the same guidance/principles, the same reusable prompts, and (when wanted) the same worktree and container isolation.
- The setup should work in two modes: starting a new project, and adding to or editing an existing one. A greenfield-only template does not cover the second mode.
- The default should be minimal. Extra machinery (diagram prompt packs, container isolation, language starters) should be opt-in rather than imposed.
- Guidance should be harness-agnostic: `AGENTS.md` is the canonical file, with any harness-specific file (for example `CLAUDE.md`) reduced to a thin pointer to it.

These govern how this tool is built.

Note: the principles the tool _ships_ to other projects (the default `AGENTS.md` content) are a separate artifact from these; the shipped set and its data model are covered in the `core-assets` step and live in `pack/principles.toml`.

## Documentation Protocol

This plan is kept current during the work.

Progress lives in the Roadmap: an ordered table of step slugs and their status (`not started`, `in progress`, `complete`, `skipped`, `next`, `optional`, `deferred`, or `blocked on <slug>`). The Roadmap is the single source of truth for status and for implementation order. The Step Details below carry each step's design, decisions, and (once done) outcome and evidence, and do not repeat the status label.

Steps are identified by a stable kebab-case slug, not a number, so a reference stays valid when the order changes: every cross-reference to a step uses its slug. Each Roadmap row maps to a detail block headed by the same slug; some detail blocks are grouped under an umbrella heading that carries context shared by several steps, and the umbrella itself is not a Roadmap entry. To add a step, add its detail block and insert its slug into the Roadmap at the right position; to reorder or reprioritise, edit the Roadmap rows. Keep the two in sync: every Roadmap slug has a detail block and vice versa.

The "Open Questions, Decisions, Issues and Blockers" section is the single living human-decision queue (the model adopted in `human-review-queue`). Each item has a stable id, a one-line ask, a status (`open`, `decided -> folded into <slug>`, or `superseded`), and a context pointer into the step or ledger that carries the detail. Resolved items are marked resolved, not deleted, so the same question is not addressed twice; the adopted decision and its reasoning are folded into the relevant step, and the queue item points to that step. The orchestrator updates the queue at every checkpoint as a required step and pushes the open items to the human rather than waiting for the human to pull them.

## Repository Layout and Current Architecture

This section maps the code as built, so the plan can be resumed without prior conversation context. The code in the repository is the source of truth; this is an index into it. The completed steps carry the reasoning; this carries the shape.

Build, run, and verify (see `README.md` for full user-facing usage):

- Use `just build` / `just test` / `just clippy` / `just fmt`, or plain `cargo` inside `nix develop` (with direnv, run `direnv allow` once). The justfile wraps the Nix environment.
- Verification before each commit: `cargo clippy --all-targets -- -D warnings`, `nix fmt`, and all text ASCII-clean (no em-dashes, en-dashes, emoji, or other non-ASCII; use `->`, `>=`, `!=`).
- The binary is a plain Rust program (edition 2021, MSRV 1.88) that runs without Nix. Nix, just, and direnv are development conveniences only. Dependencies: `clap` (derive), `serde`, `toml`, `ratatui = "0.30"`, `include_dir`.

Directory layout:

- `src/` holds the binary crate: four modules described below.
- `pack/` is the built-in default pack: `pack.toml` (the manifest), `AGENTS.md`, `plan-template.md`, `principles.toml`, and `prompts/`. It is embedded into the binary at compile time with `include_dir`, and it also loads through the same manifest path as an external `--template` pack (the pack is dogfooded).
- `flake.nix`, `.envrc`, `justfile`, `rustfmt.toml` are the dev environment (fenix toolchain, treefmt formatters, recipes). `LICENSE` is Blue Oak Model License 1.0.0.

Modules and key types (all shipped and tested):

- `src/main.rs` (CLI and orchestration). `Cli` (clap derive) exposes `--output-dir`, `--force`, `--vcs <git|none>` (git by default), `--write`, `--dry-run` (conflicts with `--write`), `--principles`, `--principle-detail`, `--list-principles`, `--template <dir>`, and `--var key=value` (repeatable); there is no `-i` flag, the selector is the default on a terminal. `main` builds the active `manifest::PackSource` (the built-in pack, or a `--template` directory), parses `--var` into an overrides map, loads the active pack's principles via `pack_principles`, and resolves the selection. `use_selector` then decides the mode: the two-pane TUI opens when both stdin and stdout are terminals, there are principles to choose, and neither `--write` nor `--dry-run` was given; its Save modal is the write confirmation. Otherwise the run is non-interactive and writes only with `--write`. Either way `main` builds the asset set with `build_assets`, computes each asset's `Outcome` with `outcome_of` (read-only, for the plan preview), decides the `--vcs` action with `init_plan` (read-only; `inside_git_repo` skips when already inside a repository), prints the plan (the repository line first), and, when confirmed, runs `run_git_init` (shell out to `git init`) before writing the assets with `apply_asset`. `Outcome` is `Created`/`Refreshed`/`SkippedExisting`/`Overwritten`; `outcome_of` decides it without touching disk and `apply_asset` performs the write, so the preview never writes. `#[cfg(test)] scaffold` is a build-plus-write convenience for the drop tests. User errors print to stderr and exit 2.
- `src/pack.rs` (principle data and selection). `Principle { id, name, summary, rationale, tags, default_selected, default_order, references, related }`. `parse_principles(&str)` parses a principles TOML file. `resolve_selection(principles, spec)` implements the `--principles` token grammar (comma-separated `default` / `all` / `none` / `tag:<t>` / bare-id, de-duplicated by first occurrence; keyword and tag tokens expand in `default_order`, id lists keep their given order) and returns `SelectionError { UnknownId, UnknownTag }`. `Detail { Name, Summary, Full }` with `render_principles` renders the selected set as a numbered list. `default_principles` / `DEFAULT_PRINCIPLES_TOML` are `#[cfg(test)]` only, because production reads principles through the active pack source (see `pack_principles`).
- `src/manifest.rs` (the pack format and loader; the abstraction `optional-modules` builds on). `PackSource { Embedded(&Dir), Directory(PathBuf) }` with a public `read(rel)` and a private `manifest()`; `builtin()` returns the embedded pack. `Manifest { asset: Vec<AssetSpec>, var: Vec<VarSpec>, module: Vec<ModuleSpec> }` deserializes `pack.toml` and ignores unknown keys. `AssetSpec { source, dest, ownership, render, module: Option<String>, executable: bool }`, `VarSpec { name, default: Option<String>, module: Option<String> }`, `ModuleSpec { name, description, guidance: Option<String>, requires: Vec<String> }`, and `Ownership { Reference, Working }`. A `module = "<name>"` tag on an asset or var declares optional-module membership: untagged entries are core (always applied), tagged ones only when `--module <name>` selects that module; the `[[module]]` section is the authoritative set of known module names. `render(template, vars)` does minimal `{{key}}` substitution (unknown placeholders left as-is). `load(source, builtin_vars, overrides, selected_modules)` reads the manifest, filters out unselected modules' assets and vars, resolves the variable map with `resolve_vars` (built-in vars first, then each selected/core declared default, then `--var` overrides; the name `principles` is reserved), and returns `Vec<Asset { dest, contents, ownership }>` or `LoadError { Io, UndeclaredVar, MissingRequiredVar, ReservedVar, UnknownModule, UndeclaredModuleTag, UndeclaredModuleRequire, DuplicateModule }` (all exit 2). The reserved computed variables are `principles`, `instrument`, and `modules` (a pack may neither declare nor `--var`-set them). The `checks` module and the `agent-scaffold checks` / `scaffold --with-precommit-hook` surfaces live in `src/checks.rs` and `src/main.rs` (see the `optional-modules` step).
- `src/tui.rs` (the interactive selector; TEA-lite, functions not traits, where TEA is The Elm Architecture: a single state value, a pure `update` reducer, and a render function). The only public item is `run_selection(principles, initial_included, save_summary) -> io::Result<Option<Vec<usize>>>`, which returns the chosen principle indices in order, or `None` on abort. Internally: one `App` value; `enum Mode { Editing, Filtering, Confirming { button } }`; `enum Event { Key, Resize }`; a pure `update(&mut App, Event) -> Step` reducer (`Step` is `Continue`/`Confirm`/`Abort`); a `ui` render function; and a blocking `run` loop over `next_event()` (the single seam where a concurrent event source could later be merged). No async, no custom traits. Key bindings are documented in the `selection-ui` step.

Pack format (the contract shared by the built-in pack and `--template` packs):

- A pack is a directory containing `pack.toml` plus the source files it references. External packs are passed with `--template <dir>`.
- `pack.toml` has `[[asset]]` entries `{ source, dest, ownership = "reference"|"working", render = true|false, module?, executable? }` (one source may map to several assets; `render` and `executable` default to false; `executable = true` drops the file with its executable bit set, for example a hook script) and optional `[[var]]` entries `{ name, default?, module? }` (an absent `default` makes the variable required), plus optional `[[module]]` entries `{ name, description, guidance?, requires? }` declaring the opt-in modules (`guidance` names a partial concatenated into `{{modules}}` when the module is enabled; `requires` lists modules it auto-enables).
- Optional modules: an asset or var with a `module = "<name>"` tag is dropped or required only when that module is selected with the repeatable `--module <name>` flag; untagged entries are core (always applied), so with no module selected the output is byte-identical to the core. Every module a tag references (and every `--module`) must be declared in a `[[module]]` section, and module names must be unique; a dangling tag, an unknown `--module`, or a duplicate declaration is an error and nothing is written.
- `ownership = "reference"` assets (conventionally under `.agents/`) are always (re)written; `ownership = "working"` assets are created only if absent, unless `--force`.
- `render = true` applies `{{name}}` substitution. `{{principles}}` and `{{instrument}}` are reserved, tool-computed variables (the rendered principle selection, and the instrumentation block when `--instrument` is set); a pack may neither declare nor `--var`-set either. Other variables come from `[[var]]` defaults, overridden by `--var key=value`; setting an undeclared variable, or leaving a required one unset, is an error and nothing is written. Every rendered asset is normalised to end with exactly one trailing newline, so an unused reserved placeholder leaves no trailing blank lines and the off output is byte-identical without relying on a formatter.
- A pack that ships `principles.toml` has its principles drive selection, `--list-principles`, the interactive selector, and `{{principles}}`; a pack without one simply has no principles to select. A pack may likewise ship an `instrument.md` render fragment, which is inlined into `{{instrument}}` when `--instrument` is set; this fragment is a special source read directly (like `principles.toml`), NOT a manifest `[[asset]]`, so it is not dropped as its own file. A pack without one renders `{{instrument}}` empty.

One run's data flow: parse CLI, build the `PackSource`, read and parse the pack's `principles.toml`, `resolve_selection` (or the interactive TUI) chooses and orders principles, `render_principles` produces the `{{principles}}` value, `manifest::load` resolves the variables and reads/renders each asset, `outcome_of` computes each asset's plan outcome, and, once writing is confirmed, `apply_asset` drops each honouring ownership and `--force`.

This section is the single living human-decision queue for the task (the model adopted in `human-review-queue`). Each item has a stable id, a one-line ask, a status, and a context pointer into the step or ledger that carries the detail. Statuses are `open` (awaiting a human decision), `decided -> folded into <slug>` (resolved; the decision and reasoning live in that step, and the item stays here marked resolved so it is not re-addressed), or `superseded` (replaced by a later item, kept for provenance). The orchestrator maintains this queue under the push-at-checkpoint rule stated in the Documentation Protocol; a folded decision reopens only on evidence that beats its recorded reasoning.

Historical note: the `OQ-<letter>` labels (OQ-A .. OQ-K) elsewhere in this plan are provenance markers for questions resolved and folded before this queue existed, not live entries; a reference such as "from OQ-E" points to a decision now recorded inline in the relevant step. The pre-queue questions were: OQ-B/C/D (selection-polish), resolved in the TUI-polish steps; OQ-E (manifest and dogfooding), OQ-F (schema and variables), OQ-G (embedding), OQ-I (variable declaration schema), OQ-J (principle source under `--template`), resolved in the bring-your-own-template steps; OQ-H (git-URL fetch), deferred to `git-url-fetch`; OQ-K (version-control init), folded into `init-vcs`; plus the earliest questions (tool form, ownership and update model, principle set and data model, non-interactive selection UI, interactive TUI design), resolved in earlier steps and the code. The live queue below uses `Q-<n>` ids.

Live queue:

Steps in implementation order, with status. The Roadmap is the single source of truth for status; the slug in each row keys the matching detail block under "Step Details". `next` marks the prioritised next work; `optional` and `deferred` mark not-started work that is not on the critical path.

## Project Principles

1. Prefer the cleaner long-term architecture over the smallest diff - prioritise correctness, internal coherence, and maintainability, and when a local fix conflicts with a cleaner design, choose the cleaner design unless a concrete limitation prevents it.
2. Minimal by default - The core does one thing well; everything else is an optional module the user opts into. Adding a module must not complicate the core.
3. Safe on existing projects - Scaffolding into a populated repo must never clobber or silently overwrite existing files. Prefer create-if-absent, namespaced locations, and clearly marked idempotent edits.
4. Idempotent - Running the tool twice produces the same result as running it once, with no duplicated blocks and no drift.
5. Make illegal states unrepresentable - Work out the valid inputs and outcomes first and encode them, rather than admitting bad states and guarding against them at runtime.
6. Ground decisions in evidence - Before an approach is built out, validate it with a small proof-of-concept that builds and produces the expected output. If the candidates are exhausted, raise the impasse for a decision rather than forcing through an unvalidated approach.
7. Reproducible - Prefer the project's existing toolchain conventions (Nix, just, direnv) so a scaffolded project and its checks behave the same on any machine.
8. Structured data first, project for humans - Prefer machine-readable structured formats as the single source for the data the workflow operates on (plan status, the decision queue, decisions/receipts, relationships, metrics), and derive human-readable views by projection, rather than hand-authoring prose that is also the machine input. At this pre-adoption stage, prioritise the best long-term design, architecture, and implementation over backwards-compatibility, API stability, or minimal-diff blast radius; those become constraints once the project has adoption and stability guarantees. Decided by the human 2026-07-18; when this conflicts with Principle 2 (minimal) or Principle 3 (safe on existing projects) at this stage, this wins, and it sharpens Principle 1 (cleaner long-term architecture) and Principle 16-equivalent one-source-of-truth thinking.

## Roadmap Status Vocabulary

Generated status vocabulary (from the code constants, so it cannot drift):

- Roadmap statuses: not started, in progress, complete, skipped, next, optional, deferred, blocked on <slug>.
- Queue statuses: open, exploring, decided, superseded.

## Open Questions, Decisions, Issues and Blockers

- `Q-1` (decided -> folded into `convergence-accounting`) convergence-accounting round accounting: how to make the escalation cap computable from the ledger. Decision and reasoning in `convergence-accounting`.
- `Q-2` (decided -> folded into `ledger-template`) ledger-template placement: where the ledger template lives. Decision and reasoning in `ledger-template`.
- `Q-3` (decided -> folded into `ledger-template`) ledger-template reference-vs-working: whether the per-task ledger copy is refreshed or create-if-absent. Decision and reasoning in `ledger-template`.
- `Q-4` (decided -> folded into `instrument-flag`) instrument-flag CLI surface: a flag or an optional module. Decision and reasoning in `instrument-flag`.
- `Q-5` (decided -> folded into `instrument-flag`) instrument-flag log location: which committed cross-task path holds the JSONL metrics log. Decision and reasoning in `instrument-flag`.
- `Q-6` (decided -> folded into `instrument-flag`) instrument-flag writer: whether the orchestrator appends JSONL directly or via a helper. Decision and reasoning in `instrument-flag`.
- `Q-7` (decided -> folded into `workflow-doc-fixes`) severity scale (finding F4): the reviewer/triager scale and which level the dismissed-finding guard triggers on. Decision and reasoning in `workflow-doc-fixes`.
- `Q-8` (decided -> folded into `deliberation-mode`) deliberation-mode approach: how to acknowledge question-driven (Socratic) human input. Decision and reasoning in `deliberation-mode`.
- `Q-9` (decided -> folded into `human-onboarding`) human-onboarding approach: how the human is onboarded and told their ongoing decision duty. Decision and reasoning in `human-onboarding`.
- `Q-10` (decided -> folded into `human-review-queue`) human-review-queue approach: where the living human-decision queue lives and how it is disciplined. Decision and reasoning in `human-review-queue`.
- `Q-11` (decided -> folded into `state-schema`) structured state file: a machine-parseable projection of progress/tasks/agents the tool can validate. Decision and reasoning in `state-schema`.
- `Q-12` (decided -> folded into `deliberation-mode`) human-input contract: what every human-input point must present. Decision and reasoning in `deliberation-mode`.
- `Q-13` (decided -> folded into `triager-independence`) triager independence: whether the triager may be collapsed into another role. Decision and reasoning in `triager-independence`.
- `Q-14` (decided -> folded into `findings-files`) findings-to-files: whether reviewers and triagers write findings to per-agent files that other agents read directly, instead of the orchestrator transcribing them. Decision and reasoning in `findings-files`; context in `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md` (post-189b7bd notes).
- `Q-15` (decided -> folded into `compaction-prep`) compaction-prep: whether to add a reusable compaction-prep user prompt and a checkpoint/resume procedure. Decision and reasoning in `compaction-prep`; context in the ledger's post-189b7bd notes.
- `Q-16` (decided -> folded into `user-prompts-dir`) user-prompts directory: whether to separate human-invoked prompts from role prompts into their own directory. Decision and reasoning in `user-prompts-dir` (which revises `human-onboarding`); context in the ledger's post-189b7bd notes.
- `Q-17` (decided -> folded into `file-safety-rules`) file-safety discipline: the git-durability-and-recovery baseline for writer agents. Decision and reasoning in `file-safety-rules`; the two motivating incidents are in the ledger's post-189b7bd notes.
- `Q-18` (decided -> folded into `agent-isolation`) isolation tiering: whether to run writer agents under capability-tiered isolation. Decision and reasoning in `agent-isolation`; context in the ledger's post-189b7bd notes.
- `Q-19` (decided -> folded into `compaction-prep`) resume user prompt: a reusable prompt to continue the in-progress task after a compaction, the pickup counterpart to `compaction-prep`'s flush. Decision and reasoning in `compaction-prep`; the prompt lives in `.agents/user-prompts/` per `user-prompts-dir`.
- `Q-20` (decided -> folded into `gate-prompt-clarity`) gate-prompt clarity: `clarifying-questions.md` and `open-questions-gate.md` are agent-run human-decision gates (the "me" in them is the human), not agent-only role prompts; their "ask me" wording is imprecise about the sub-agent -> orchestrator -> human routing, and the README labels them "one prompt per workflow role" though they are planner-phase gates. Decided: keep them as the planner's gate prompts (do not fold into `planner.md`), fix the routing wording, and correct the README label. Recovered from the transcript ([07-11T10:17] "answer at the gates"); a compaction had flattened "human-decision gate" into "agent-facing role prompt". Decision and reasoning in `gate-prompt-clarity`.
- `Q-21` (decided -> folded into `human-review-queue`) between-step checkpoint cadence: how the orchestrator behaves at a step boundary. Decided (human, `(d)`+`(b)`): configurable at kickoff, defaulting to report-and-continue (the orchestrator reports what completed and what is next, then continues; the human can opt into gating each boundary, and can interrupt via intake at any time). Decision and reasoning in `human-review-queue`.
- `Q-22` (decided -> folded into `no-wrap-convention`) prose line-wrapping and the formatter: whether agents police line length, and whether the tooling owns wrapping. Decided (human): no manual hard-wrapping and line length is never a review finding (a workflow convention plus a reviewer/triager rule); and adopt `prettier` `proseWrap: never` via a repo-root `.prettierrc.json` so the existing `nix fmt` markdown formatter actively unwraps prose and owns wrapping (accepting a one-time repo-wide reflow). Decision and reasoning in `no-wrap-convention`; user preference also in memory (`no-hard-wrap-prose`).
- `Q-23` (decided -> folded into `human-review-queue`) compaction lifecycle discoverability in onboarding: the "Getting started, for the human" section did not mention the compaction-prep / resume prompts. Decided (human, thin pointer): add a short pointer in Getting started naming `.agents/user-prompts/compaction-prep.md` (paste before a compaction) and `resume.md` (paste to continue after one), no duplication of the procedure; land it as part of `human-review-queue`. Decision and reasoning in `human-review-queue`.
- `Q-24` (decided -> folded into `state-schema`) instrumentation data integrity: the `--instrument` metrics log is written by the orchestrator (an LLM) as hand-authored JSONL (`Q-6`), so records are not guaranteed well-formed. Decided (human): guarantee structure by DETECTION, not prevention, the `state-schema` `validate` subcommand checks `docs/metrics/workflow.jsonl` against a schema defined once (the record types and fields fixed in `pack/instrument.md`), reporting or rejecting malformed records, so the data is deterministically verifiable and bad lines are discardable. `instrument-flag` keeps its tool-free direct-write (no runtime dependency on the binary during a workflow run, honouring the harness-agnostic constraint), so `Q-6` is NOT reopened. A validated-append writer (prevention) was considered and declined because it would require the `agent-scaffold` binary on PATH at workflow-run time. Decision and reasoning in `state-schema`.
- `Q-25` (decided -> folded into `optional-modules`) module design: the `[[module]]` schema, the module set, and the deterministic-checks module. Decided (human, design pass): a hybrid schema (asset `module = "<name>"` tags + a `[[module]]` metadata section) with a `--module` flag; the priority modules are a deterministic-checks module (a user-configurable, pluggable lint/format gate, `ast-grep` first-party, implementer applies formatters while a read-only `checks-reviewer` runs linters and check-mode formatters into a findings file the triager adjudicates) and the isolation module (agent-box/agent-images pointer setup), built machinery -> checks -> isolation. Decision and reasoning in `optional-modules`.
- `Q-26` (decided -> folded into `optional-modules`) deterministic-checks module config format: how a project declares its checks so `ast-grep` is first-party but other linters/formatters plug in. Decided (human, 2026-07-16, resurfaced post-compaction): option (a), a declarative `.agents/checks.toml` with `[[check]]` entries naming a command, a kind (lint/format), and a format check-mode command, pre-populated with ast-grep + the formatter, users add entries (one source of truth, pluggable, harness-agnostic, no tool dependency). Declined: (b) reuse justfile recipes (depends on `just`, couples to that module) and (c) ast-grep native `sgconfig.yml` + guidance (less pluggable for non-ast-grep tools). Unblocks building increment 2 of `optional-modules`; the settled config layout lives in `optional-modules`. Decision and reasoning fold into `optional-modules`.
- `Q-27` (decided -> folded into `workflow-invariants`) deterministic workflow-invariant enforcement: a deterministic check so a skipped or unreviewed step fails rather than relying on the orchestrator's discretion (motivating incident 2026-07-16: the `pause.md` step committed outside the review loop, which no check would have caught). Decision and reasoning in `workflow-invariants`; design record in `docs/plans/workflow-invariants.explorations/`.
- `Q-28` (decided -> folded into `state-queries`) workflow-state navigation/query commands: whether to add targeted read commands (e.g. `agent-scaffold state`, `agent-scaffold next`) that emit a slice of the ledger RESUME STATE, the current step, the open-questions items, and the next required action, for token-efficient resume instead of reading the whole plan and ledger. Trade-off: a token win only where the source grows unbounded or is re-read (the ledger especially), against per-call and per-turn overhead that can exceed a single file read for small files, plus a projection-drift risk (mitigated by the existing structural validator). Decided (human, 2026-07-16, after the two-explorer design pass): build a thin `next` plus a `status --resume` slice (NOT a separate `state` verb, NOT a wholesale navigator), scoped to the resume and CI/check paths only (the honest token win is at cold resume and in a hook, and is negative per-turn during active work, so do not poll it each turn); re-scoped by `round-log-core` (`Q-34`) so it reads the plan parser (`src/plan.rs`) plus the JSONL round log (`src/metrics.rs`) plus a verbatim `## RESUME STATE` section extract, NOT a `ledger-parse` (that step was skipped; there is no `src/ledger.rs`), and reuse `workflow-invariants`' next-action transition logic. `next` doubles as the enforcement aid. Folded into `state-queries`.
- `Q-29` (decided -> folded into `exploration-mode`) first-class support for design-space exploration / deliberation: the workflow handles deciding (the `deliberation-mode` human-input contract) and deferring (an item stays `open`) a decision, but has no first-class mode for exploring a design space BEFORE options are decidable, which is what the human asked for on `Q-27`/`Q-28`. Gaps the incident exposed: (1) the Open-Questions status vocabulary (`open` / `decided -> folded` / `superseded`) cannot express "open, a design pass is owed first" (the `validate --plan` structural check hard-rejected that phrasing when tried in the status field), so the nuance can only live in body prose; (2) there is no defined deliverable for an exploration (implementation has an artifact, a plan has plan-review, an exploration has neither, so it lives as ad-hoc orchestrator prose); (3) the exploration itself is unreviewed, though it is higher-leverage than implementation; and (4) intake names factual-answer and decision-gate modes but not "the human is asking me to deliberate", so handling falls to orchestrator disposition. Decided (human, 2026-07-16, after the two-explorer design pass): add all four, low-ceremony, mostly docs plus one code line: an `exploring` Open-Questions status (in `plan.rs`), a design-notes artifact convention at `docs/plans/<task>.explorations/<question>.md` (parallel to `.reviews/`) whose shape is the human-input contract written to a file, design-exploration as a named intake mode in `AGENTS.md`, and an optional light review of an exploration (the multi-independent-explorer-plus-orchestrator-synthesis setup that produced this very decision is the review analog). Same "structure over disposition" root cause as `Q-27`/`Q-28`. Folded into `exploration-mode` (built first, being the cheapest and unblocking proper deliberation).
- `Q-30` (decided -> folded into `test-driven`) test-driven process module: optional module adding a per-step test spec and a test-first phase before implementation. Decision and reasoning in `test-driven`; design record in `docs/plans/test-modules.explorations/`. Related: `Q-31`.
- `Q-31` (decided -> folded into `mutation`) mutation-testing module: an optional module layered on top of `test-driven` that mutates the code and confirms the tests kill the mutants (tests are not vacuous). Decision and reasoning in `mutation`; design record in `docs/plans/test-modules.explorations/`.
- `Q-32` (decided -> folded into `session-preflight`) session preflight and adherence confirmation: the workflow has no defined moment where the orchestrator confirms, with the human, the process, the disciplines it requires, and the isolation modes/tools available, so adherence is assumed rather than established (the `pause.md` skip and the untested isolation both trace to this). Decided (human, 2026-07-16): add a preflight step the orchestrator runs before the workflow starts (at kickoff) and whenever it is resumed (post-compaction or post-pause), in which it reviews the workflow process and its disciplines, detects and states the available writer-isolation modes and tools (container via `agent-box`/`agent-images` if wired, else git worktree, else the file-safety fallback), and confirms with the human what it will do to adhere before proceeding. Also folds in the human's standing directive to ALWAYS use separate, isolated (worktree-first) writer agents where possible, so the orchestrator drives and reviews but does not implement code directly. Folded into `session-preflight`.
- `Q-33` (decided -> folded into `optional-modules`) real agent-box/agent-images integration for our own spawned agents (vs pointer scaffolding): whether `optional-modules` increment 3 grows a real container-integration path or stays pointer-scaffolding. Decision and reasoning in `optional-modules` (increment 3); design record in `docs/plans/cross-harness-isolation.explorations/`.
- `Q-34` (decided -> folded into `round-log-core`) unify round/review data on the JSONL log (single source of truth, comprehensive): evidence found while scoping `ledger-parse` (2026-07-16): structured per-round data is DUPLICATED across the ledger's `## Round summaries` table (stale at 2 rows) and the metrics JSONL (comprehensive, 52 rows, richer: `risk_class`, reviewer attribution, severities, `ts`, cross-task), violating single-source (Principle 16). DIRECTION DECIDED (human, Approach 1): promote `docs/metrics/workflow.jsonl` to the always-present structured workflow ROUND LOG (round logging becomes core, not opt-in), REMOVE the ledger's round-summary and findings-index tables (the ledger keeps only the Round-records narrative + RESUME STATE), and have convergence accounting, `workflow-invariants`, and `state-queries` all read the JSONL; per-finding detail stays in the review files (git history). The human accepts backwards-compat breaks for the optimal long-term shape. This REOPENS folded decisions (`instrument-flag` `Q-4`/`Q-5`/`Q-6` the `--instrument` opt-in gate; `ledger-template` `Q-2`/`Q-3` the tables; `convergence-accounting` `Q-1` counting from the ledger; `Q-24` metrics validation now core) and re-scopes `ledger-parse` (down to a RESUME-STATE text extract, likely folded into `state-queries`) and `workflow-invariants` (reads the JSONL). A DESIGN PASS is owed (human-chosen) to pressure-test Approach 1 and produce the concrete MIGRATION plan: the `--instrument` disposition (drop vs repurpose), the always-on round-log rendering (the `instrument.md` fragment becomes core AGENTS.md content), the ledger-template edits, the convergence-accounting doc change, which existing steps reopen and in what order to keep the repo green, and the (minimal) data migration since the 52-record JSONL already covers all rounds. GATES the remaining process cluster (`ledger-parse`/`workflow-invariants`/`state-queries`) until the migration plan is decided.
- `Q-35` (decided -> folded into `review-mode`) human-invokable code-review mode, integrated as a fourth entry mode: a human prompts the agent to review the current tree or a diff against a plan and/or constraints, and gets a findings report. Decision and reasoning in `review-mode`; design record in `docs/plans/review-mode.explorations/`.
- `Q-36` (decided -> folded into `reviewer-diversity`) cross-harness / cross-model agent spawning: generalise reviewer/triager diversity beyond one harness and let the orchestrator spawn agents on different models or CLIs to minimise shared blind spots. Decision and reasoning in `reviewer-diversity` (adopt-now docs rule) and `optional-modules` increment 3 (deferred spawn-map); design record in `docs/plans/cross-harness-isolation.explorations/`.
- `Q-37` (decided -> folded into `reviewer-harness-field`) record the reviewer's harness in the round-log attribution: after `reviewer-diversity` generalised the diversity rule to "models or harnesses", a one-off interim read of the metrics log (2026-07-17) found the per-reviewer `reviewers[]` object carries only `{role, model, raw_findings, valid_findings}`, so a future cross-harness proof-of-concept could not be measured (no harness recorded). Decided (human, 2026-07-17): add an optional `harness` string to each `reviewers[]` element, before any cross-harness work. Decision and reasoning in `reviewer-harness-field`.
- `Q-38` (decided -> folded into `optional-modules`) seed a ripgrep ASCII check and dogfood ASCII enforcement: the checks module's seeded ast-grep rule cannot express this project's ASCII-clean convention (ast-grep rules must match AST kinds, not raw bytes), so a text/regex tool is needed. Decided (human, 2026-07-18): (a) NOW, ship a COMMENTED ripgrep `ascii-clean` example row in the seeded `checks.toml` (a non-ast-grep text/regex linter forbidding non-ASCII bytes, demonstrating the plug-in story and giving the ASCII recipe without imposing it on unicode-using projects); done in `optional-modules` increment 2b alongside a commented `ruff`+`paths` polyglot example. (b) DEFERRED to AFTER increment 2c: fully dogfood ASCII enforcement in agent-scaffold itself by adopting the checks module (`scaffold-self --module checks`) with an ACTIVE ripgrep ASCII row, once the `checks` subcommand exists to run it. Tracked as a post-2c action.
- `Q-39` (decided -> folded into `optional-modules`) format-check execution and isolation under the checks module: treefmt v2 (this project's `nix fmt`) has NO non-mutating dry-run, so a `kind = "format"` check via `--fail-on-change` FORMATS IN PLACE then reports; this would mutate the user's working tree (and, in a pre-commit hook, risk clobbering unstaged changes). 2b corrected the WORDING (the checks-reviewer authors only findings; a format check on an in-place formatter mutates a discardable copy). DECIDED (human, 2026-07-18): the `agent-scaffold checks` subcommand runs the checks in a SEPARATE, TEMPORARY git worktree (never the live tree), removed when the run finishes (including on failure/interrupt), so the current tree is never clobbered. This makes in-place format checks safe and matches the project's git-is-the-recovery-substrate and writer-isolation model. Content selection: a plain `checks` run isolates the current WORKING-TREE state (e.g. via a `git stash create` commit of the dirty state, or HEAD when clean); `checks --staged` isolates the STAGED/index content (e.g. a tree written from the index), so the pre-commit hook checks exactly what will be committed without touching unstaged work. Implementation plumbing (git primitives, edge cases: empty repo, no commits, clean tree) is worked out in 2c and verified by its risky review loop.
- `Q-40` (decided -> folded into `waiver-model`) representing a human-authorized convergence below the required clean-round streak so `validate --workflow` (W3) does not false-flag it: DECIDED (human, 2026-07-18) the escalation-record-aware approach (W3 accepts an increment's recorded streak when its records carry a `type:"escalation"` `human_decision:"decision"` record; reuses existing log data, no new schema/status, scoped and auditable). RESOLVED and built in `waiver-model` (2026-07-18): the escalation-record-aware idea was refined into the unified waiver, an explicit `accepted-at-escalation` `record-backed` `type:"waiver"` record whose `evidence` W5 validates against the increment's real `type:"escalation" human_decision:"decision"` record (rather than W3 silently auto-accepting on escalation presence), so the exemption is a declared, unit-scoped, auditable log line. Decision and outcome are in the `waiver-model` Step Detail. `optional-modules` increment 2c-ii was RISKY (its review found three high-severity write-escapes) but converged at ONE clean round, not two, because at the 5-round total-cap the human decided (per the escalation contract) to accept it, the dangerous write-escape class being closed and independently confirmed. Its round records are therefore risky with a peak `consecutive_clean` of 1, which W3 (risky requires peak >= 2) will flag as non-converged when `optional-modules` is eventually marked `complete` (after increment 3). Neither `trivial` nor `grandfathered` fits (it is neither review-skipped nor pre-logging). MUST be resolved before `optional-modules` is marked complete. Candidate approaches to bring to the human: an escalation-decision-aware exemption in W3 (a `type:"escalation"` record with `human_decision:"decision"` for the increment authorises its recorded streak); a per-increment override marker/status; or record the human-accepted lower streak as an explicit accepted-risk annotation W3 reads. Related to the severity-trajectory convergence idea recorded in `workflow-calibration`. This gap was surfaced by this very session's dogfooding (the meta-value of the workflow reviewing itself).
- `Q-41` (decided -> folded into `optional-modules`) `optional-modules` increment 3 (the isolation module) concrete file shape: the DIRECTION is already decided (pointer-scaffolding, not real container integration; `Q-33`/`Q-36`, design record `docs/plans/cross-harness-isolation.explorations/q33-q36-{A,B}.md`), but the step detail owes "its own design pass" on the exact integration shape, which files the `--module isolation` bundle drops and how they point a project at agent-box (`https://github.com/0xferrous/agent-box`) and agent-images (`https://github.com/nothingnesses/agent-images`) for worktree/container writer-agent isolation. A design pass is owed; the item points at `docs/plans/optional-modules-inc3.explorations/`. Grounding for the pass: the checks module (increment 2) is the template for how a content module lives in the pack (a `[[module]]` with a `guidance` partial rendered into `{{modules}}`, plus module-tagged assets, create-if-absent working files vs reference files); agent-box uses a global `~/.agent-box.toml` plus `ab spawn --local` and `ab new`/`ab spawn -s <session> --git` (worktree mode) over agent-images OCI images. The deferred growth path (a `.agents/spawn.toml` role->launch map + an agent-box container-wrapper shim) is already recorded in the step detail and stays deferred; this pass settles only the pointer-scaffolding content that ships now. Design pass DONE (two explorers, `docs/plans/optional-modules-inc3.explorations/isolation-module-shape-{A,B}.md`). Both converge on the skeleton: one `[[module]] isolation` entry with `guidance = "isolation-guidance.md"` and no `requires` (orthogonal to checks), one guidance partial `pack/isolation-guidance.md` rendered into `{{modules}}` (points at the `agent-isolation` rule for the tier policy, supplies only the container-tier wiring: agent-images `nix build`/`podman load`, the global `~/.agent-box.toml` shape, the `ab new`/`ab spawn --git` worktree flow, and preflight detection), byte-identical when off, no runtime dependency, no shelling out. They DIFFER on ONE point, the open decision: whether to ALSO drop a project-local working file `.agents/isolation/README.md` (create-if-absent). Explorer A (minimalism/safety): NO, guidance-only, because agent-box config is a per-user GLOBAL file so there is nothing project-local to own and a dropped README duplicates the partial's content (drift, Principle 16); zero clobber surface (Principle 3). Explorer B (reproducibility): YES, one lean working file recording WHICH image the project uses (e.g. claude-code vs codex), a project-level fact captured nowhere else in the repo, so setup is not tribal knowledge (Principle 7). DECIDED (human, 2026-07-18): Option A, GUIDANCE-ONLY, no dropped file. Rationale: agent-box config is a per-user global file so there is nothing project-local to own; the one project-level fact (image choice) is thin and a scaffolded template risks an empty stub or duplicating the partial (Principle 16); it is the safest shape (Principle 3, zero clobber surface) and lands the setup where the orchestrator already reads it at preflight (Principle 1). Folded into the `optional-modules` increment-3 step detail.
- `Q-42` (decided -> folded into `decision-receipt`) a checkable receipt that the human-input contract was presented at every human-decision point: the human observed (2026-07-18) that the `Q-40` decision was acceptable because they were presented the options, trade-offs, a recommendation, and principle-judged reasoning before deciding, and asked that the workflow GUARANTEE this every time a human decides, with a durable receipt/proof that it happened. The human-input-contract RULE already requires this presentation; what is missing is a checkable receipt and enforcement (the same rule-then-detection pattern `workflow-invariants`/W3 applied to convergence). Partial receipts already exist (`type:"escalation"` and `type:"intake"` records; decided Open-Questions items folded into steps), so this generalises them. Key design nuance for genuine PROOF vs orchestrator self-attestation: the strongest receipt records the OPTIONS PRESENTED plus the human's SELECTION among them (choosing option Y from {X,Y,Z} inherently proves {X,Y,Z} was shown), rather than a self-certified "I presented the contract" flag. Candidate approaches to weigh: (a) a new `type:"decision"` JSONL receipt (question id, options presented, recommendation, human's choice) plus a `validate --workflow` check (a W4 analog) that every `decided` queue item has a matching receipt, coherent with the round-log/ledger split (Principle 16 with the accepted round-vs-narrative duplication, Principle 6 checkable); (b) a lighter in-queue standard: every `decided` item must carry the presented options + the choice inline, with a completeness check over the prose (less machinery, weaker check); (c) rely on git history + the existing escalation/intake records + AskUserQuestion structured answers, no new mechanism (Principle 2 minimal, but no deterministic proof, under-delivers on the ask). COUPLES to `escalation-exempt` (`Q-40`): escalation acceptance is itself a human decision that would carry a receipt, and both make W3 read human-decision records, so `escalation-exempt`'s build is held until this path is chosen so the two are designed coherently. The design space is genuinely open (receipt form, which decision points are covered, what makes proof genuine, whether to enforce), so a design pass (explorers) is a candidate before deciding. Trade-off the human should weigh: accountability/auditability of the decision process vs ceremony added to every decision point (Principle 2). A DESIGN PASS is owed (human-directed, 2026-07-18); the item points at `docs/plans/decision-receipts.explorations/`. The human is unclear on four things the pass must answer head-on: (1) the PURPOSE and what it AFFORDS the workflow (for whom, and what auditability of the decision process actually buys); (2) the TRADE-OFFS (the Principle 2 ceremony cost vs the benefit); (3) what it would concretely LOOK LIKE (form, where it lives, the check); and (4) how it RELATES to the plan, especially the Open Questions section and Principle 16 (single source of truth), is a receipt a duplicate of the decided queue item (an SSOT violation) or a genuinely distinct thing (the ATTESTATION that the contract-shaped presentation happened, vs the queue which holds the decision CONTENT). FIXED CONSTRAINT handed to the pass (established in deliberation, not re-open): receipts do NOT live in the ledger, because the ledger is per-task and DELETED at task close while decisions are permanent, and most decisions happen outside any review loop where no ledger exists; the ledger only narrates a mid-loop decision (a cap escalation, an accept-risk) as a POINTER to the durable receipt/queue, exactly today's escalation pattern. The NULL option (existing machinery, the Open Questions queue + `type:"escalation"`/`type:"intake"` records + git history + structured AskUserQuestion answers, already suffices, build nothing or only a light check) is a first-class candidate the pass must genuinely weigh, not a strawman. DESIGN PASS DONE (two explorers, `docs/plans/decision-receipts.explorations/receipt-system-{A,B}.md`; A skeptical/minimalist recommends NULL, B architect/steelman recommends the FULL receipt). They AGREE on three things that narrow the choice: (i) the LIGHT option (a prose-completeness check on queue items) is rejected by BOTH (a structural check over free prose parses at the wrong boundary, Principle 14, and is weaker than it looks); (ii) the CEILING, no orchestrator-authored artifact is adversarial PROOF, only auditable evidence, since the same orchestrator writes both the presentation and the receipt (the same ceiling W3 already accepts); the only truly unforgeable evidence is the human's own AskUserQuestion selection, which lives in the transcript/git outside the workflow artifacts; (iii) `escalation-exempt` (`Q-40`) is UNBLOCKED either way, it reads the existing `type:"escalation"` record for its W3 exemption regardless of this decision, and a decision receipt (if built) is a separate record for a separate check. So the real choice is a clean binary. FULL (B): a permanent `type:"decision"` JSONL record (`q_id`, `options`, `recommendation`, `chosen` with the validator enforcing `chosen` is a member of `options`, `ts`) written when a queue item goes `decided`, plus a W4 `validate --workflow` check that every `decided` item has a matching receipt (forward-looking, historical Q-1..Q-41 exempt). Genuineness comes from the options-on-record + `chosen in options`; SSOT resolved as queue=CONTENT (what/why) vs receipt=PROCESS ATTESTATION (options+choice), q_id-linked, the same accepted split as the ledger-narrative vs JSONL-round-record. NULL (A): build nothing; a well-formed `decided` queue item ALREADY records options+recommendation+choice+reasoning in prose (`Q-41` is the existence proof), so a JSONL receipt is a SECOND permanent hand-authored home for the option-labels+choice -> Principle 16 drift with only a soft resolver; and since the orchestrator authors both, the receipt is no more trustworthy than the queue it duplicates, so it fails Principle 3 (no motivating incident; adds no independent trust). A's crux: W3's justification does NOT transfer, W3 guards a SILENT unsupervised skip, but a human is PRESENT at every decision, so a missing presentation is not silent. Optional A add-on: one AGENTS.md sentence making "a `decided` item records the options presented and the human's choice inline" the item standard, no new machinery. Orchestrator recommendation: leaning FULL (B) with A's constraints folded in (record only option LABELS + choice, never the reasoning, so the duplication is bounded and the queue stays authoritative for content; frame it honestly as enforced auditability, NOT proof), because the project's own revealed philosophy is enforce-over-trust (W3, the metrics validator, the drift guards, the ASCII check all exist for exactly this) and the human asked for a checkable guarantee; the honest counterweight is A's permanent-double-authoring SSOT cost and that the receipt cannot prove presentation, only that a structured record exists. NOTE for whoever builds it: B's document is internally inconsistent on whether to add `q_id` to `escalation`/`intake` records (its main text says add it; its YAGNI says do not and keep them separate); resolve toward KEEPING them separate (one purpose per record, Principle 16), a `decided` queue item gets its own decision receipt. HUMAN CHOSE FULL over NULL (2026-07-18): build a checkable decision receipt. BUT the human then noticed the SSOT hole A poked (a JSONL receipt is a second permanent home for the option-labels+choice) and proposed encoding the receipt as STRUCTURED DATA projected into the human-readable plan, so it is single-sourced. So the FULL direction is chosen, but the receipt's ENCODING/HOME is REOPENED as `Q-43`; the build waits on it. This item is resolved on direction only; the encoding decision is `Q-43`. RESOLVED: built as the `decision-receipt` step (Q-44 pilot 1); status updated to folded there.
- `Q-43` (superseded by `Q-44`) how the (chosen) Full decision receipt is ENCODED and where it lives, plus the broader structured-plan idea it touches: the human chose to build a receipt (`Q-42`) but flagged that Explorer B's raw JSONL record is a second permanent hand-authored home for the option-labels+choice (the Principle 16 drift A poked), and proposed encoding the receipt as STRUCTURED DATA with a single home, then having `agent-scaffold` PROJECT it into the human-readable plan/status view. Three contender encodings to weigh: (a) B's `type:"decision"` JSONL record (matches existing instrumentation records, opt-in, leaves the core plan format untouched, but is the second home); (b) a structured block on the `decided` Open-Questions item in the plan (single home, co-located, but complicates `src/plan.rs` and changes the scaffolded plan format for every project); (c) structured receipt source + a projection/render into the human-readable view (single source, derive the view, closes the SSOT hole AND keeps the plan readable; costs a render capability that OVERLAPS the deferred `workflow-viz`). So weigh the full rewrite on its own merits separately; the receipt only needs the narrow structured-receipt-plus-projection win. It weighs ONLY the three encodings (a)/(b)/(c) for the already-chosen Full receipt, and is explicitly SCOPED OUT of the full-plan-rewrite (that larger question is noted here, not designed). Fixed inputs the pass may not re-open: the Full receipt is chosen (not null); the receipt records the options presented + recommendation + the human's choice with a check that the choice is among the options, plus a W4 `validate --workflow` check that every `decided` item has a receipt (forward-looking); receipts do NOT live in the ledger; `escalation`/`intake` records stay separate (one purpose per record). The pass must give concrete COST ACCOUNTING per encoding: `src/plan.rs` parser changes, `src/metrics.rs` changes, the W4 check in `src/workflow.rs`, the pack plan-template FORMAT change and its blast radius across every scaffolded project, and for (c) exactly how much projection/render machinery is needed and whether it forces the deferred `workflow-viz` to come first or a thin render suffices. It should note the existing `status --json` plan->JSON projection as prior art for (c). They CONVERGE far more than they differ: BOTH reject (b) (the in-plan structured block: 60-100 lines of multi-line parser in `src/plan.rs` past its deliberate no-prose boundary, and an UNCONDITIONAL `pack/plan-template.md` queue-item format change every scaffolded project inherits whether or not it ever records a decision, Principles 2+3), and BOTH agree the receipt SUBSTRATE is identical: a `type:"decision"` JSONL record (`q_id`, `options`, `recommendation`, `chosen` with a validator check that `chosen` is a member of `options`, optional `ts`), a new `metrics::parse_decisions` projection, a W4 `validate --workflow` check that every `QUEUE_FOLD_PREFIX` (`decided`) item has a matching `q_id` receipt (forward-looking, historical Q-1..Q-41 exempt via the log's absence), `src/plan.rs` and the plan-template BYTE-IDENTICAL, plus the CONVENTION that a `decided` item's prose does NOT re-enumerate the option labels (the receipt is their machine home; the prose carries rationale + the pointer), stated in `pack/AGENTS.md`/`pack/plan-template.md`. The SOLE difference is a ~30-35 line thin RENDER in the existing `status` command (a `DecisionProjection` slot in `Projection`, populated from `parse_decisions`, shown in `status --json` and the human summary): A says defer it (it is (a)-plus-sugar, does not itself touch the queue prose, and belongs in the planned `state-queries` step that already extends `status`); B says build it now because it creates a derived DISPLAY surface that makes the no-re-enumerate convention SELF-REINFORCING (the orchestrator, seeing options+choice already shown by `status`, has no pull to hand-copy them into prose), and BOTH note it naturally folds into `state-queries`. It is NOT the deferred `workflow-viz` (a live streaming Gantt over a dispatch/return event log, different scope); it is a one-time read of committed data like `status` already does. Orchestrator recommendation: build the SUBSTRATE (JSONL `type:"decision"` record + `chosen in options` + W4 + drift-guard + `instrument.md` docs + the convention) as a new `decision-receipt` step now (classify RISKY, it extends the enforcement backstop like `workflow-invariants`/W3 did -> two clean rounds), and FOLD the thin `status` render into the already-planned `state-queries` step where both explorers agree it belongs (keeping this increment tight; the receipt+W4 is the guarantee, the render is human-convenience, and its interim absence only means options+choice are read from the JSONL until `state-queries` lands). Awaiting the human's confirmation of that split, or a choice to bundle the render into the receipt increment now. The broader full-structured-plan-format idea remains recorded here and out of scope, to weigh separately. The decision-receipt encoding is therefore no longer a standalone minimal-footprint question; it becomes the FIRST INSTANCE of the broader architecture now opened as `Q-44`. The receipt-encoding explorations (`receipt-encoding-{A,B}.md`) still inform it (the receipt fields, the W4 check, and the reject-(b)-multi-line-parser reasoning survive; but (b)/(c)'s blast-radius penalty is now demoted). - `Q-44` (open) the structured-data + human-projection ARCHITECTURE, and the cleanup of accumulated sharp edges: the human decided (2026-07-18, Project Principle 8) that the target architecture is structured, machine-readable formats as the single source for the workflow's data, with `agent-scaffold` projecting them into human-readable views, prioritising best long-term architecture over backwards-compat/blast-radius at this pre-adoption stage. The human ALSO asked to clean up and refine sharp edges in the current design/implementation as part of this, naming the `grandfathered` status as an example. Two coupled questions the human posed: WHAT should be encoded as structured data, and HOW (format + projection). Orchestrator initial framing (to be worked out in a design pass, not decided here): the metrics JSONL is the existing model; the natural structured skeleton is the Roadmap (status + order + dependencies), the Open Questions queue (id/status/ask/options/choice/receipt), decisions/receipts, and cross-references; the hard part is the Step Details reasoning narrative (~80% of the plan), which likely stays PROSE PAYLOADS linked from the structured skeleton (structured frontmatter/records + Markdown bodies) rather than being forced into data fields; `agent-scaffold` renders the structured source -> the human-readable plan/status view (extending the existing `status --json`/`--resume` projection, the prior art). Candidate SHARP EDGES to audit and refine under the architecture-first mandate (initial, non-exhaustive): (1) the `grandfathered` and `trivial` W3-exemption statuses (special-case escape hatches on the Roadmap status enum); (2) the PROLIFERATION of W3 exemption mechanisms, `grandfathered` + `trivial` + the proposed `escalation-exempt` (`Q-40`) would be a THIRD kind of below-bar convergence exemption, which likely should be UNIFIED rather than each a special case (so `escalation-exempt` should be reconsidered as part of this, not built as a one-off); (3) the plan as Markdown-with-parsed-regions vs a structured source (the core of this pivot); (4) the ledger round-narrative vs the JSONL round-record duplication; (5) the hand-maintained `## RESUME STATE` and Status line (candidates to derive by projection); (6) the alphanumeric `-inc<x>` leading-slug strip in W3; (7) the soft prose/JSONL duplication of decision option-labels (the very thing that reopened the receipt question). This is a large, governing initiative that reprioritises the near-term one-off builds (the decision receipt, `escalation-exempt`, the checks/isolation dogfood): those should be re-planned to fit the target architecture rather than built first. A DESIGN PASS (and likely an AUDIT to enumerate the sharp edges rigorously) is owed before any build; the SCOPE and staging are the human's to set. APPROACH DECIDED (human, 2026-07-18): AUDIT + DESIGN PASS, then STAGE. Phase 1 is a read-only AUDIT (multiple parallel auditors -> findings docs under `docs/plans/architecture-audit.explorations/`) mapping the current data model (what is structured vs prose, what parses it, what is hand-maintained or duplicated) and enumerating the sharp edges. Phase 2 is a DESIGN PASS on the target architecture (what to encode, the format, the projection/render, the migration path, how the exemptions unify and the edges resolve) synthesised into a target design + a STAGED roadmap. Phase 3 is the staged build in reviewed increments, with the decision-receipt as the PILOT first instance validating the architecture (Principle 6). PHASE 1 AUDIT DONE (three read-only auditors, `docs/plans/architecture-audit.explorations/audit-{data-model,enforcement-exemptions,sharp-edges}.md`). Synthesis: (1) DATA MODEL, only four things are machine-parsed today (the plan Roadmap table, the Open-Questions id/status/ask, the Step-Detail heading slugs, and the JSONL records); everything else in the plan and the ENTIRE ledger is prose no code reads. (2) ENFORCEMENT, eleven special-cases; the headline is that `grandfathered` + `trivial` + the proposed `escalation-exempt` are ONE concept (an authorised waiver of the convergence bar) wearing three hats, unifiable into one waiver notion carrying {unit: step-or-increment, reason: predates-logging|review-skipped|accepted-at-escalation, evidence tier}, with TWO evidence tiers so cleanup does not launder a weak self-declaration into looking as strong as an independent escalation record. `optional-modules` is LIVE-STUCK at `in progress` because of this (cannot mark complete without a false W3 flag). Load-bearing invariants any refactor must preserve: the pause.md catch (an undeclared complete-with-no-evidence step must still fail), exemptions stay declared+visible, the escalation scope (decision-only, one increment, streak-shortfall-only), and per-increment convergence semantics. (3) INDEPENDENT SHARP EDGES not resolved by the structured-data pivot, to schedule as a separate cleanup track: SE-1/SE-2/SE-17 naming collisions (two Principle namespaces cited without a namespace prefix; `trivial` names both a Roadmap status and a metrics Classification variant); SE-3 the two-tier workflow (a non-instrumented project gets NO machine enforcement and `validate --workflow` silently false-greens, significant); SE-7 `instrument.md` is an undocumented magic pack filename; SE-8 `ModuleSpec.description` dead field; SE-9 `checks` Test/Mutation kinds parse but silently skip; SE-6 the drift-guard checks field-name presence but not required/optional status. They CONVERGE on the substance and split on ONE axis. AGREED by all three (the settled core): (i) the decision-receipt substrate (already converged: `type:"decision"` JSONL {q_id, options, recommendation, chosen [validate chosen in options], ts} + W4); (ii) the UNIFIED WAIVER model, one concept absorbing grandfathered+trivial+escalation carrying {unit: step|increment, reason: predates-logging|review-skipped|accepted-at-escalation, evidence-tier: self-declared|record-backed} with the record-backed tier VALIDATED against a real `type:"escalation" human_decision:"decision"` record (a new W5), plus a single `baseline` marker replacing the 14 grandfathered rows and per-check historical exemptions; it preserves the pause.md catch, escalation scope, per-increment convergence, and the two tiers with no laundering; (iii) an explicit step/increment structured link replacing the lexical `-inc<x>` strip; (iv) the two PILOTS, INC 1 = decision receipt, INC 2 = the waiver model + W5 (which UNSTICKS the live-stuck `optional-modules` via a record-backed waiver pointing at its escalation record), both risky, both pure-additive first; (v) prose stays prose linked by slug/q_id; (vi) the projection extends `status`/`state-queries`, is read-only, and is NOT the deferred `workflow-viz`; (vii) escalation/intake stay separate records and `skipped` stays a distinct status; (viii) the independent sharp edges are a parallel backlog. THE ONE SPLIT is how far to structure the HUMAN-AUTHORED SKELETON (Roadmap status, queue, Status line, RESUME STATE): A INCREMENTAL keeps the Roadmap table + queue as human-edited Markdown regions and moves only machine-event data to the JSONL log (lowest churn/risk; honest residual, P8 met only for event data not the skeleton, Status line deleted-or-drift-checked); C DX/EVENT-SOURCED event-sources ALL mutable state (status, streak, lifecycle, decisions, waivers) into the append-only JSONL and derives every status/Status-line/RESUME-STATE, keeping prose Markdown directly editable with `### <slug>:` headings as the skeleton, NO round-tripping (the tool never writes the authored files, never parses rich Markdown back), reaching P8's substance with the best LLM authoring ergonomics (append is the cheapest/safest write) and deleting the whole round-trip/clobber failure class, at the cost of a bigger core and authors no longer hand-writing statuses; B CLEAN-SLATE puts the whole skeleton in a `<task>.plan.toml` with prose in ~92 Markdown sidecars and a NEW `agent-scaffold render` that GENERATES a committed-but-never-hand-edited `<task>.md`, reaching P8's form fully but at real cost (a render engine of several hundred lines, three-location editing, a required render-before-commit step, agent-scaffold-on-PATH becomes required, and a generated-file-must-not-be-hand-edited footgun especially bad for a dogfooded LLM-authored system). Orchestrator recommendation: the two AGREED pilots (receipt, then waiver) are low-regret and high-value (they unblock `optional-modules`) and should be built NOW; they are literally the append-then-derive-then-check pattern C is built on, so they pilot the deepest architecture on the smallest surface (Principle 6). DEFER the final A-vs-C-vs-B skeleton-depth decision until after the pilots give evidence; orchestrator lean for that later call is C (it reaches P8's substance with the best ergonomics and no round-trip failure class, where B pays a large render-engine + editing-regression + footgun cost for more structural form, and A leaves P8 only partial), with A as the conservative fallback. DECIDED (human, 2026-07-18): PILOTS NOW, decide the skeleton depth AFTER (evidence-first, Principle 6). PLACEMENT DECIDED (human, 2026-07-19): ALL of this doc/prompt currency work lands INSIDE Inc 6 of `structured-skeleton`, accepting the larger single-increment diff for one fully coherent, fully current pack shipment. Options presented and their trade-offs: (a) SPLIT, put the coherence-critical prompts (`orchestrator.md`/`planner.md`, the ones a fresh scaffold reads first) into Inc 6 and defer the rest (`README.md`, `pack/AGENTS.md`, `pack/instrument.md`) to a separate follow-on docs-sync step, smallest Inc 6 diff and fastest coherence for the load-bearing prompts but ships a scaffold whose README/docs still lag until the follow-on lands (Principle 4 small-change bias, at the cost of a temporary partial incoherence); (b) ALL INSIDE INC 6, one increment ships a fully coherent and current pack with no lag window, at the cost of a larger Inc 6 diff (Principle 16 one-source coherence, Principle 8 the pack matches the architecture it ships); (c) SEPARATE STEP AFTER INC 6, keeps Inc 6 tight around the template-and-scaffold mechanism but leaves the shipped scaffold incoherent between Inc 6 and the follow-on and adds a whole extra Roadmap step for what is editing (Principle 4 at the cost of a lingering incoherent scaffold and more ceremony). Orchestrator recommendation was (a), the split, to keep Inc 6's already-risky diff smaller and land the coherence-critical prompts first. Human chose (b), all-inside-Inc-6, for a single coherent pack shipment: the increment that changes the template and scaffold is the same increment that makes every prompt and doc describe the flow it ships, so the pack is never internally inconsistent. RAISED as part of Q-47's doc/prompt currency work: Q-47 decided all currency edits land in Inc 6, but the canonical `pack/AGENTS.md` phase-2 wording is not a mechanical filename swap; it changes what the canonical guidance ASSERTS about how a plan is authored (from "fill in a Markdown template" to "edit a TOML skeleton plus prose sidecars, then render a generated view you must never hand-edit, re-rendering before every commit"), so it was recorded here for the human rather than decided by the planner. Options presented and their trade-offs: (A) keep `pack/AGENTS.md` phase 2 ABSTRACT, describe planning as producing a structured plan skeleton projected into a human-readable view, and let the planner prompt, the README, and the shipped `plan-template.plan.toml` carry the concrete TOML/render/sidecar mechanics; preserves the doc's rule-not-mechanism altitude and keeps it stable if the projection encoding ever changes, but leaves the canonical guidance one level removed from what the scaffold actually ships and risks the abstract description drifting from the concrete prompts (a coherence gap of its own, against the whole point of this currency pass). (B) describe the TOML/render/sidecar flow DIRECTLY in `pack/AGENTS.md` phase 2 as the scaffolded default; makes the canonical guidance concretely match the shipped scaffold (Principle 16 one-source coherence, Principle 8 the doc matches the architecture it ships) and is consistent with the doc's EXISTING altitude, which already names concrete scaffolded artifacts throughout (`.agents/prompts/`, `.agents/LEDGER.template.md`, `docs/metrics/workflow.jsonl`, the `type:"decision"` record schema, and today the `TEMPLATE.md` filename it is replacing), so it is harness-agnostic but not mechanism-agnostic; the cost is that a future non-TOML projection would need an AGENTS.md edit, and the phase-2 description carries a little more procedure. Reasoning judged against the Principles: Principle 16 (one source of truth) and Principle 8 (the cleaner long-term architecture, here the pack describing the architecture it ships) favour (B); Principle 17's harness-agnostic generality is the pull toward (A), but AGENTS.md is already mechanism-concrete, so (A) would be a new abstraction the doc does not currently maintain elsewhere.
