- `Q-49` (decided -> folded into `doc-redundancy-cleanup`) cross-document redundancy between the pack docs/prompts and the canonical `pack/AGENTS.md`, raised by the human on Principle 8's one-source-of-truth clause and routed audit-first: DECIDED (human, 2026-07-19) SINGLE-SOURCE-WITH-REFERENCES: state each canonical rule/constant once in `pack/AGENTS.md` and replace type-A copies with a pointer, leave the type-B role prose as is, and fix the type-C accuracy bugs, chosen over leaving the copies duplicated and over generating the pack from `AGENTS.md`. Decision, ranked targets, and reasoning in `doc-redundancy-cleanup`; the audit files are the detailed evidence. Receipt: `Q-49`.
- `Q-50` (decided -> folded into `doc-currency-guidance`) whether keeping documentation and prompts current with a shipped change is an optional module, a deterministic check alone, or core workflow guidance: DECIDED (human, 2026-07-19) make documentation currency a CORE CROSS-CUTTING guidance reusing the existing roles, a documentation-impact assessment in the PLANNER's duties (when folding a change, identify which docs/prompts it makes stale) and in `pack/AGENTS.md` phase 2, plus a doc-currency check at the ACCEPTANCE step (does the shipped change leave any doc or prompt stale?), with the implementer updating and the reviewer verifying; chosen over an opt-in `--module` bundle and over a deterministic check alone. It complements, and is a prerequisite for, the workflow-driver's later doc-generation stages (`Q-51`). Decision and reasoning in `doc-currency-guidance`. Receipt: `Q-50`.
- `Q-51` (decided -> folded into `workflow-driver`) encode the workflow itself as a deterministic state-machine DRIVER (not just a post-hoc checker) to combat AGENT DRIFT as the workflow grows and accrues detail (agents forget to apply rules): the human proposed (2026-07-19) making `agent-scaffold` a Mealy-machine REPL that the orchestrator (or any agent/human) runs to keep driving the loop. It would (a) parse the current codebase + plan/ledger/metrics state into valid internal control states, (b) print a summary of the current state, the valid transitions it can take, and CRUCIALLY the next INSTRUCTION PROMPT for the agent (the role prompt with context filled in) based on that state, plus periodic reminders (for example the Project Principles). Documentation instructs agents to run it to receive further instructions, so the control flow lives in a deterministic program rather than in fallible agent memory. This is the natural third stage of the structured-data direction (structured SOURCE `plan.toml` -> structured ENFORCEMENT `validate --workflow` -> structured DRIVING, this idea) and builds directly on the machine-readable plan the `structured-skeleton` initiative just delivered. ORCHESTRATOR REFRAMING (to weigh in the design pass): it is not one global Mealy machine but a FLEET of per-unit control FSMs (one per active step / increment / review loop), the tool tracking the set and emitting the next instruction for each READY unit, and also acting as a DEPENDENCY SCHEDULER over the plan's `blocked_by` DAG (computing what may run in parallel now); the tool must own only CONTROL transitions and consume agent/human JUDGMENT as inputs (a triage verdict, a human decision, a commit), never make the judgments itself (get that boundary wrong and it either straitjackets or hallucinates decisions). HUMAN'S CONCERNS to resolve: (1) interaction with the writer-isolation tiers (orthogonal: the tool instructs, the orchestrator isolates; the tool reads durable state on main and can emit the resolved tier as part of the instruction); (2) parallel steps (the per-unit-FSM fleet + dependency scheduling above); (3) concrete inputs and outputs (candidate: input = the durable state plus event-recording subcommands like `record-round --outcome clean` that append the log AND advance state atomically, Principle 13; output = a per-unit state summary + valid transitions + the filled next-instruction prompt + Principle reminders, as JSON and human text; keep it STATELESS, recompute from the files each call so it survives crashes/compaction); (4) what is missing. ADDITIONAL CONSIDERATIONS the orchestrator raises for the pass: the tool would become the SOURCE of the workflow LOGIC (convergence/sequencing move from AGENTS.md prose into Rust), the strongest anti-drift property but the biggest cost, and the prose and code could themselves drift unless the AGENTS.md workflow section is GENERATED from the machine definition (the same render closure just built for the plan); ESCAPE HATCHES for exceptions and human overrides are mandatory or the tool becomes a cage; ADOPTION should be EVIDENCE-FIRST (Principle 6), start ADVISORY (the agent drives, the tool suggests, measure whether drift drops) then deepen to authoritative driving, mirroring the receipt/waiver pilots; and this OVERLAPS the queued drift-mitigation cluster (the re-grounding + provenance step and the `Q-50` doc-currency core guidance), so the pass must weigh whether the driver SUBSUMES them. This is a MAJOR initiative, larger than `structured-skeleton`, and genuinely design-open, so it is `exploring`: the human directed (2026-07-19) queueing the exploration FIRST, before folding the Q-50 / re-grounding work it overlaps. A multi-explorer design pass is owed (candidate lenses: a formal-FSM + concurrency model; the tool-vs-prose single-source boundary + AGENTS.md generation; the I/O contract + isolation/harness integration + advisory-first adoption; and a YAGNI/skeptic lens weighing the state-machine driver against the lighter drift mitigations), synthesised, then moved to `open` with options for the human. Design record: `docs/plans/mealy-workflow-driver.explorations/`. DESIGN PASS DONE (2026-07-19): four independent explorers (fsm-concurrency, tool-vs-prose, io-contract-and-adoption, yagni-skeptic) synthesised in `synthesis.md`. STRONG CONVERGENCE: build the ADVISORY MVP first (a read-only, stateless `agent-scaffold next` reusing the W3/W4/W5 reconstruction already shipped, largely the already-planned `state-queries` step plus a next-action reminder), DEFER the full driver (per-unit FSM fleet, `blocked_by` scheduler, event-recording write-path, AGENTS.md-from-machine generation, authoritative driving) behind an EVIDENCE GATE, single-source the convergence CONSTANTS to close the existing `required_streak`/AGENTS.md duplication, and treat the driver as COMPLEMENTING (not subsuming) the re-grounding step and Q-50 (un-gate those; Q-50 is a prerequisite if the driver is built). Options for the human: (A) build the advisory MVP evidence-gated [all four explorers recommend]; (B) build the full driver now [rejected as premature, a subsystem larger than structured-skeleton hardening an evolving workflow]; (C) do not build, rely on the lighter drift mitigations. Orchestrator recommendation: (A). HUMAN DIRECTION (2026-07-19): go with the FULL DRIVER (Option B) as the TARGET, but a DEEPER design pass is owed FIRST, brainstorm the design space, requirements, scope, and possible user flows before committing to a build, so this stays `exploring` (a second design pass owed, not yet decided). Orchestrator note carried into that pass (honouring the skeptic's main objection while enabling the full driver): keep the workflow LOGIC DATA-DRIVEN (a spec the tool interprets, not hardcoded Rust) so the workflow can still evolve without heavy code churn, and STAGE the build (the advisory tier is the natural first increment on the path to the full driver, reusing the W3/`state-queries`/`render` machinery). ROUND 2 exploration queued (lenses: requirements + scope; user flows / end-to-end scenarios; design-space breadth / fullest vision + staging; architecture + incremental build path). On synthesis, present a fuller design + a concrete staged build plan for the human to refine, then decide with a receipt. Round-2 design record: `docs/plans/mealy-workflow-driver.explorations/` (r2-* + r2-synthesis.md). DESIGN SHAPE DECIDED (2026-07-19): ORQ-1 terminal authority = ADVISORY + validated event-RECORDING, NON-BLOCKING in-loop, with an OPT-IN boundary gate (`validate --workflow --strict` in CI or a pre-commit hook, reusing the checks/hook pattern) for hard enforcement at the LANDING boundary (this also answers ORQ-2: gate at the boundary, not per-step, keeping the loop robust, harness-agnostic, least-authority, and Q-24-respecting). ORQ-3 (multi-repo) defaults to single-project (far-stage). ORQ-4 (spec-change ceremony): `workflow.toml` is load-bearing config, so edits go through the normal plan + review like AGENTS.md changes, no new ceremony. ORQ-5 (reminder cadence): light, each `next` surfaces only the reminders relevant to the current action. READY TO FOLD: a `workflow-driver` staged umbrella (Stage 0a the `workflow.toml` control-constants spec + single-source the convergence constants into W3, closing the `required_streak`/AGENTS.md duplication; 0b generate the AGENTS.md control fragment from the spec, byte-guarded; 1 the advisory read-only `next` MVP, ~the planned `state-queries` + a next-action reminder; 2 type the `src/driver/` FSM engine with a shared reconstructor; 3+ the `blocked_by` scheduler, generation-widening, the guarded `record-*` write-path [reopens Q-24], and the opt-in authoritative boundary gate, all evidence-gated) carrying the decided design (data-driven-constants spec + Rust transition function; the projection-family framing where `validate`=backward, `status`=summary, `next`=forward, `render`=prose; the control-vs-judgment boundary enforced by types; semi-authoritative non-blocking recording). Q-51 is a DECIDED item once folded, with a receipt: the human chose the FULL DRIVER (staged) over the four explorers' advisory-MVP-only recommendation. Receipt: `Q-51`.
- `Q-52` (exploring) a deterministic, statistical-evidence-backed way to determine whether code is EARNING ITS KEEP (useful and worth keeping) vs accrued cruft/maintenance burden, run periodically (manually or in the workflow, e.g. at the acceptance/UAT step), likely MULTIPLE methods working together: the human raised (2026-07-19) that AI-accelerated development accrues code and maintenance burden faster, making keep-or-cut discipline the bottleneck, and asked for the prior art and how to OBJECTIVELY determine code usefulness. ORCHESTRATOR STARTING ANSWER (to be verified and extended by a WebSearch-backed research pass, since knowledge has a cutoff): usefulness is not intrinsic but RELATIONAL, made objective by making it FALSIFIABLE, the direct analog of mutation testing (mutation asks 'if I change this, does a test catch it?'; usefulness asks 'if I REMOVE this, does anything observably notice?'). Operational definition: code earns its keep iff removing it causes an observable loss (a test fails, the build breaks, a downstream dependency breaks, a production metric/behavior changes, or a documented requirement / public-API contract is violated). PRIOR-ART CLUSTERS to survey and cite: (1) static unused-code/reachability detection (compiler/linter dead-code, call-graph, `cargo-udeps`/`cargo-machete`, vulture, staticcheck, Knip, tree-shaking), blind to reflection/FFI/dynamic-dispatch/public-API; (2) dynamic/production usage (coverage as necessary-not-sufficient, PRODUCTION coverage / 'dark code' sampling, feature-usage telemetry tying code to user value); (3) DEBLOATING research (CHISEL, RAZOR, TRIMMER, piecewise compilation; delta-debugging / 1-minimality, Zeller), operationalizing keep-what-observed-use-needs; (4) mutation testing and its INVERSE, 'pseudo-tested methods' (Niedermayr et al.: body-nulling survives all tests -> effectively untested/weak-value), generalizable to a DELETION experiment; (5) behavioral-code-analysis / maintenance-cost hotspots (Tornhill/CodeScene churn x complexity, Feathers), the burden side; (6) traceability/provenance (code mapping to no requirement/test/API contract is suspect; connects to this repo's provenance direction). MULTI-SIGNAL DESIGN to propose: combine reachable/depended-on? (static) + exercised? (coverage) + deletion-safe? (deletion/pseudo-test mutant survives tests + canary, the strong signal) + traceable? (requirement/API contract; public API a legit keeper) + cost>value? (hotspot) into an ADVISORY report of candidates-not-earning-their-keep with per-signal evidence, fed into the triage/implement loop where a human decides each deletion (NEVER auto-delete; Chesterton's Fence; public-crate API surface excluded or deprecation-cycled). WORKFLOW FIT: run periodically at acceptance/UAT or on a cadence, reusing the `checks` module (already runs lint/format/test/mutation kinds in an isolated worktree) + the `review` entry mode; the deletion experiment maps onto the planned mutation module. This is `exploring`: the human directed (2026-07-19) recording it now and running the research pass AFTER the Q-51 Mealy exploration synthesises. A WebSearch-backed research pass is owed (lenses: a prior-art survey with citations; the concrete multi-signal design + `checks`/`review`/`mutation` composition; a skeptic lens on false-positives, the public-API/semver problem, and the auditor's own maintenance cost). Design record: `docs/plans/code-value-audit.explorations/`.
- `Q-53` (decided -> folded into `task-entry-regrounding`) whether to add a task-entry re-grounding and human alignment check before starting a step: DECIDED (Socratic, human, 2026-07-19) add a LIGHT, stakes-scaled task-entry RE-GROUNDING run before starting a step and on resume, sourced from the DURABLE artifacts (plan Step Detail by slug, ledger RESUME STATE, decision receipts by q_id, findings by path, code by file:line/commit) and NOT conversation history (a fact found only in ephemeral history is a durability bug to flush into the artifacts, not to cite; out-of-VCS history pointers were rejected as moving the fragility rather than fixing it): the orchestrator writes a short brief (what the task is, why it exists, the cited evidence including the decision receipt with the human's recorded choice, and what it is about to do) and pushes it for a go/no-go via the human-input contract, scaled to stakes. Also ADOPT structured PER-STEP PROVENANCE links (a step -> the decisions/findings/commits that justify it, mirroring questions' `folded_into`/`receipt`) that `render` can present as "why this step exists". Chosen over no-regrounding and over out-of-VCS history pointers. Decision and reasoning in `task-entry-regrounding`. Receipt: `Q-53`.
- `Q-54` (decided -> folded into `human-input-gate-reinforce`) the human-input contract (present options with their trade-offs, a recommendation, and reasoning judged against the numbered Project Principles, and let the human decide) lives once in `pack/AGENTS.md`, but the orchestrator drifted off it after a compaction and presented several decision gates as options-with-trade-offs while omitting the explicit recommendation and Principle-judged reasoning; how to prevent recurrence. This is control-drift (an agent forgot a rule that applies right here), not a missing rule, so the fix is point-of-action reinforcement. DECIDED (human, 2026-07-19): reinforce at the point of action via (a) strengthening `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md` so the human-input contract is an explicit per-gate checklist the orchestrator applies at EVERY decision presentation (referencing, not restating, the AGENTS.md contract, per Principle 8), built first as its own low-risk step; and (b) adding an awaiting-human-decision reminder to the Stage-1 driver `next` output (folded into the `workflow-driver` Stage 1 scope), which is the point-of-action anti-drift value the driver is designed to deliver, so this incident is live evidence for it (Principle 6). Chosen over folding the whole fix into Stage 1 (delays the recurrence-preventer), a note-only capture, and a deterministic check (the presence of a recommendation is not reliably machine-checkable). Decision and reasoning in `human-input-gate-reinforce`. Receipt: `Q-54`.

## Step Details

Decisions carried from the resolved open questions:

- **Form.** A standalone Rust binary (clap CLI plus a ratatui TUI) that runs without Nix; Nix is a development-only dependency (the dev shell). Distributed via `cargo install` or prebuilt binaries, with an optional flake app for Nix users. Runs from any working directory and writes to any target via `--output-dir`, defaulting to the current directory with a namespaced layout.
- **Ownership (two-tier).** Tool-owned reference assets under `.agents/` are always refreshed; user working files (the root `AGENTS.md`, plans under `docs/plans/`) are create-if-absent, with `--force` to overwrite. An existing `AGENTS.md` is left untouched and the namespaced reference copy is refreshed for the user to merge from. Marked-block co-tenancy and a 3-way-merge `update` were considered and deferred (`later-enhancements`).
- **Template source.** A built-in default pack, with optional `--template <local-path>` for bring-your-own packs (`byo-template`); git-URL fetch is deferred to `git-url-fetch` (must not depend on Nix) and a flake-ref stays out of scope.
- **Principle data.** Structured TOML (`pack/principles.toml`). Each principle has `id`, `name`, `summary`, `rationale`, `tags` (category tags plus applicability tags `universal`/`static-types`/`fp`/`oop`), `default_selected`, `default_order`, and optional `references`/`related`. The selected set renders as a numbered list ordered by `default_order`, at `name`, `summary`, or `full` detail. The default set is about two dozen principles, applicability-tagged so it adapts to the project type (see `pack/principles.toml` for the live set and counts); the shipped prompts and guidance stay principle-agnostic and defer to the selected set.
- **Optional modules (not yet built).** A diagram prompt pack, a justfile of recipes, language starters, and container/worktree isolation. Each is defined concretely in `optional-modules`; the isolation module would integrate the separate agent-box (`https://github.com/0xferrous/agent-box`) and agent-images (`https://github.com/nothingnesses/agent-images`) projects, which are not part of this repository.

### `core-assets`: Author the minimal core assets and principle data

Wrote the canonical `AGENTS.md` guidance (with a `{{principles}}` placeholder), the plan-document template, the three core prompts, and the principle data as structured TOML (initially 48 principles, 21 default; see `pack/principles.toml` for the current set, which has grown since), parsed and tested. Content policy: the shipped prompts and guidance stay principle-agnostic and defer to the user-selected principle set in `AGENTS.md`, rather than baking in specific opinions a user may not have chosen (the open-questions gate was corrected to follow this, and the prompts instruct the agent to read `AGENTS.md` to align with the current principles). The workflow (in `AGENTS.md`) is role-separated so no agent grades its own work, and each role has its own prompt in `.agents/prompts/`: `orchestrator.md` (drives the phases, spawns the roles, routes context, runs the review loop, escalates), `planner.md` (drafts the plan; with `clarifying-questions.md` and `open-questions-gate.md`), `reviewer.md` (independent reviewers, assume-issues, given the change context via before and after commit hashes or a diff range, diverse lenses and models where available), `triager.md` (adjudicates each finding, never the agent that produced the artifact, may run a short producer-versus-reviewer debate on a contested finding), and `implementer.md`. Where the harness lacks sub-agents, one agent plays the roles in sequence but records each role's output. Implementation iterates over the plan's Roadmap steps (implement, review to convergence, mark the step complete), and the workflow stops only when every step is complete and an acceptance review (reviewers, then a triager, as in the other review phases) confirms the plan's Success Criteria. The orchestrator's convergence rule ends a single review loop after the required consecutive clean rounds (scaled to stakes, since fresh reviewers make one clean round weak evidence, and a dismissed high-severity finding is re-checked by a second triager before it counts towards a clean round); it escalates to a human, then resumes, if the artifact reaches a total-round cap (default five). Escalation is a decision request, not a stop. The two round constants (the required consecutive clean rounds and the total-round cap of five) are provisional defaults chosen to be safe rather than measured, to be calibrated against data from real use (see `workflow-calibration`). A human may also interrupt at any point with new or changed requests; the orchestrator first runs a bounded intake assessment that advises on impact and routing and gives feedback on the request (the human decides), folding a trivial request in directly and routing anything that touches scope or Success Criteria to the planner, so non-trivial human input always enters durably through the plan rather than ad hoc. Relitigation is prevented by a review ledger (each finding, its verdict, reasoning, and action, plus each round's outcome) handed to each round under a "no re-raise without new evidence" rule; the ledger is kept in a file committed to version control beside its plan so it survives the orchestrator losing context and travels across machines and sessions, deleted (deletion committed) at task close, kept out of the Open Questions section, and only durable decisions fold into the plan's steps (reopened only by evidence that beats the recorded reasoning). The plan template's Project Principles section (and the `AGENTS.md` Plan step that points at it) instructs the agent to seed a plan's principles from the `AGENTS.md` principles in order, add the project-specific ones after, consolidate any overlap into a single amended principle, and keep the list numbered so it can be referenced by number.

### `file-dropper`: Proof-of-concept the file-dropper

The Rust binary drops the asset set under `--output-dir`, refreshing tool-owned reference assets under `.agents/`, creating absent working files (root `AGENTS.md`, `docs/plans/TEMPLATE.md`), and leaving existing working files untouched unless `--force` is given. `AGENTS.md` is generated by rendering the selected principles into the pack guidance template. Covered by unit tests.

### `idempotency-safety`: Idempotency and safety pass

Re-run refreshes reference assets and skips existing working files (tests cover create, refresh-versus-skip, and `--force` overwrite); `--output-dir` targets any directory.

Write safety and default mode (added later): writes are off unless confirmed, and the interactive selector is the default on a terminal. A run always builds the asset set and prints a plan preview (a present-tense line per asset: `create` / `refresh` / `skip (exists)` / `overwrite`). The mode is chosen by `use_selector`: when both stdin and stdout are terminals, there are principles to choose, and neither `--write` nor `--dry-run` was given, the two-pane selector opens and its Save modal is the confirmation (Save writes, Cancel or abort writes nothing). `--write` takes the non-interactive path and applies the changes (using `--principles`), skipping the selector; `--dry-run` prints the plan and exits without writing (it conflicts with `--write`); and with no terminal and no `--write` (a pipe or CI) the run previews and writes nothing. There is no `-i` flag and no y/N prompt; the selector's modal replaced the prompt. `--force` stays orthogonal (it decides overwrite-versus-skip for existing working files; `--write` decides whether to write at all). Implementation: `outcome_of` computes each asset's outcome without touching the filesystem (so the preview never writes), and `apply_asset` performs the write; terminal detection uses `std::io::IsTerminal` (no new dependency). Validated by a unit test that planning creates nothing, plus functional runs of the no-flags preview, `--write` (byte-identical to the pre-change golden), `--dry-run`, and the `--dry-run`-plus-`--write` conflict (exit 2). The auto-launch of the selector on a terminal needs a manual TTY run (not automatable here).

### `selection-ui`: Selection UI

The non-interactive path: `--principles default|all|none|<ids>` selects the set and `--principle-detail name|summary|full` sets the rendering, both feeding generation and `--list-principles`. A non-interactive route and a recorded selection always remain available so the tool stays scriptable and idempotent.

The interactive TUI (ratatui, `src/tui.rs`) is built and, since the later default-mode change, opens by default on an interactive terminal (there is no `-i` flag; `--write` and `--dry-run` take the non-interactive path), seeded from the resolved `--principles` set. First-pass design as built:

- **Scope: principles only.** Toggle inclusion and reorder principles. Modules are out of this pass, since they are specified but not yet built (`optional-modules`); a module pane or mode is added when modules exist, reusing this pass's interaction code.
- **Layout: two-pane.** Left pane lists available (not included) principles; right pane lists included principles (numbered) in their chosen order. The focused pane is marked by border colour and the cursor highlight; the cursor wraps at both ends of a pane.
- **Key bindings.** `i`/`a` move the highlighted principle to the other pane, inserting it before (`i`) or after (`a`) that pane's cursor, with the cursor following the moved item. (This replaced an earlier Space/Shift+Space scheme: Shift+Space is not distinguishable from Space without the enhanced keyboard protocol, which is unreliable, for example on Alacritty; plain letter keys work everywhere and match vim's insert/append.) `Tab`/`BackTab`/left/right/`h`/`l` switch focus; up/down or `j`/`k` move the cursor; with the Included pane focused, Shift+up/down or `K`/`J` reorder the highlighted principle, overriding `default_order`. `u` undoes and `U` redoes. `/` opens the Available filter (type to narrow by name/id/tag, Enter applies and keeps it, Esc clears it; added in `available-filter`). `Enter` opens the save modal; `q` aborts, and Esc aborts from editing but cancels the modal.
- **Undo/redo.** `u`/`U` step through whole-state snapshots. Each editing action (`i`/`a` move, `K`/`J` reorder) checkpoints the pre-edit state before mutating; a fresh edit clears the redo stack. Navigation (cursor, focus) does not checkpoint, so undo steps through edits rather than movement. Snapshots are valid states, so undo/redo cannot break the disjoint-partition invariant.
- **Detail footer.** A footer shows the highlighted principle's `name`, `summary`, and `tags`. No search/filter in this pass (the few dozen principles scroll fine); filter is a clean later follow-up.
- **Save modal.** `Enter` is labelled save and opens a modal stating what saving will do (target directory, which files are created or refreshed, whether `--force` overwrites, and the included count) with Save and Cancel buttons. Focus defaults to Cancel so an accidental confirm never writes; left/right (or Tab/`h`/`l`) moves between buttons, Enter activates, Esc cancels. The modal is modal: editing keys are ignored while it is open. Choosing Save runs the scaffold and prints the ready-to-paste `--principles <id1,id2,...>` line (in the chosen order) for non-interactive replay; Cancel returns to editing; aborting writes nothing. `main` supplies the modal's summary lines (the file set is static; the target and `--force` behaviour come from the flags).
- **Dependencies.** `ratatui = "0.30"` (0.30.2 latest) with default features (`all-widgets`, `crossterm`, `macros`); the umbrella crate re-exports the workspace split (`ratatui-core`, `ratatui-crossterm`, `ratatui-widgets`), so no sub-crates are added directly. Events use the bundled crossterm (0.29) via `ratatui::crossterm`, not a separate dependency. Terminal lifecycle uses `ratatui::init()`/`restore()`; `init()` installs a panic hook that restores the terminal, so no hand-rolled hook is needed. A blocking `event::read()` loop is used, not async/tokio. MSRV is 1.88 (satisfied by the flake's `fenix-monthly latest`); the crate stays on edition 2021. void-installer (an earlier ratatui 0.28 TUI prototype in a separate repository, not part of this one) was assessed and not reused: it has no toggle/reorder/multi-pane code and its binary is broken against its own WIP API; only the 0.28 terminal-lifecycle and `List`/`ListState` idioms carried over, and those are current in 0.30.
- **Internals (TEA-lite, functions not traits).** The selector is one concrete `App` value with the Elm split expressed as plain functions: `update(&mut App, Event) -> Step` (a pure-ish reducer, no IO, where `Step` is `Continue`/`Confirm`/`Abort`; unit-tested for move/reorder/undo/redo/save-modal/abort), `ui(&mut Frame, &mut App)` (a render function of state, no `Widget` impl on `App`), and a thin `run()` that wires read to update to draw. No custom traits and no generic harness; the reducer is tested by calling `update` with synthesised events, and ratatui's `Backend`/`TestBackend` is available for render tests, so no bespoke `OutputSink` is needed. This keeps the separation of concerns and testability that void-installer's generic trait framework was reaching for, without the generics.
- **Concurrency (sync now, one seam for later).** A blocking loop over a small `enum Event { Key(KeyEvent), Resize }` fed by one `next_event()` that currently wraps `event::read()` (filtering to key-press and resize events). No async, no tokio, no channel. `next_event()` is the single upgrade point: if a real concurrent producer ever lands (for example an isolation step streaming progress, or a live preview pane), it becomes a `std::sync::mpsc` receiver merging sources, still synchronous, without touching `update`/`ui`. This is void-installer's event-source decoupling minus its async runtime; full async stays off the table for a scaffolder.
- **Order round-trips (gap closed).** Reordering overrides `default_order`. `resolve_selection` was re-sorting every result through `ordered_by_default`, so an explicit id list lost the user's order; it now keeps the given order (only `default`/`all` sort by `default_order`), so the printed `--principles` line reproduces the TUI result. The `selection_modes_resolve` test asserts list order accordingly.

### TUI polish and tag-based selection

Shared context for the four steps below (`mode-enum`, `tag-selection`, `available-filter`, `include-all-visible`); this umbrella is not itself a Roadmap entry. These are near-term selector improvements that build on the shipped TUI; each reuses the existing `App`/`update`/`ui` structure and the `next_event` seam. The sub-steps are ordered so each is validated before the next depends on it.

Decisions adopted from the resolved open questions:

- **Modes (from OQ-B).** A `Mode` enum (`Editing`, `Filtering`, `Confirming { button }`) replaces the `confirming` bool and `confirm_button`, so the modes are mutually exclusive by construction (make illegal states unrepresentable) and later modes do not multiply bools. The applied filter query lives on `App` (not inside `Filtering`) because its narrowing of the Available pane persists back in `Editing`; `Filtering` just means keystrokes edit that query, which also keeps `Mode` `Copy`.
- **Filter (from OQ-C).** The filter narrows the Available pane only, live and incremental, a case-insensitive substring over name, id, and tags, with a hand-rolled query string (no new dependency) and a visible-to-underlying index projection for the cursor and toggle. Included is never filtered, so reordering is never over a partial view.
- **Tags (from OQ-D).** `--principles` accepts `tag:<t>` tokens (bare tokens stay ids; `default`/`all`/`none` unchanged); each tag expands in `default_order`, the whole list is de-duplicated by first occurrence, and an unknown tag returns `SelectionError::UnknownTag`. Interactive tag selection reuses the filter (which matches tags), with an optional include-all-visible action on top.

Each sub-step below carries the evidence-grounding discipline: validate the adopted approach with a proof-of-concept (build plus tests plus, where relevant, a functional run); on failure fall back to the recorded next-best approach; if all are exhausted, raise the impasse rather than force an unvalidated approach.

#### `mode-enum`: Mode enum refactor

Replaced `confirming: bool` and `confirm_button` with `enum Mode { Editing, Confirming { button: Button } }`; `update` dispatches on `app.mode` and the focused button exists only inside `Confirming`. The `Filtering` variant is introduced in `available-filter`, where it is first used, so every commit stays free of unused-variant warnings (`available-filter` added it as a unit variant with the query on `App`). Validated by proof-of-concept: the 24 existing selector tests pass unchanged (retargeted from the old bool to `Mode`) and behaviour is identical (open/save/cancel, editing keys ignored while confirming); clippy `-D warnings` clean. The fallback (keep the bool with a `debug_assert`) was not needed.

#### `tag-selection`: Non-interactive tag selection

`resolve_selection` now treats `--principles` as a token list where each token is `default`/`all`/`none`, `tag:<t>`, or a bare id; tokens expand (keywords and tags in `default_order`, ids to themselves) and concatenate, de-duplicated by first occurrence, so a bare id list still preserves its round-trip order. `tag:<t>` naming a tag no principle carries returns `SelectionError::UnknownTag`. Validated by proof-of-concept: unit tests for tag expansion, keyword/id/tag composition with dedup and default-set-first ordering, unknown-tag error, a guard that no id collides with a reserved token, and the unchanged all/none/default/id-list behaviour; plus functional runs (`--principles tag:fp --list-principles` lists the two fp principles in order; `--principles tag:bogus` errors with exit 2). The fallback (a separate `--tags` flag) was not needed. (Since the binary now reads `tags`, the `Principle` struct's blanket `dead_code` allowance was narrowed to the still-unused `related` field.)

#### `available-filter`: Interactive Available filter

`/` enters `Mode::Filtering`; the Available pane narrows live to a case-insensitive substring over name, id, and tags; typing appends to the query, Backspace deletes, Enter applies and returns to `Editing` (filter kept), Esc clears and returns. The Available cursor and toggle map through a visible-to-underlying projection (`available_visible`/`available_display`); Included is never filtered. Refinement: an item returned to Available while a filter is active is appended (its pool position is not user-meaningful, and "before/after cursor" is ambiguous under a projection); with no filter, behaviour is byte-for-byte unchanged, so all prior toggle tests pass. Validated by proof-of-concept: unit tests on the projection narrowing and persistence, tag matching, modality (editing keys type into the query), backspace/esc editing, and toggle-under-filter moving the correct principle and preserving the partition; clippy `-D warnings` clean, 33 tests pass. The interactive feel still wants a manual TTY run (not automatable here). The fallback (jump-to-first-match) was not needed.

#### `include-all-visible`: Optional include-all-visible

Skipped by decision. A key to move every currently-visible Available match into Included at once (tag-based bulk selection on top of the filter). Skipped because the `available-filter` `/` filter already makes finding and adding matches quick (filter, then `i`/`a` on each), so bulk-add is not needed now; it stays minimal-by-default and can be revisited if adding many tagged principles at once becomes common. No `A` binding was added; the `selection-ui` key-bindings reference already includes `/` from `available-filter`.

### Bring-your-own template

Shared context for the three steps below (`pack-manifest`, `external-packs`, `pack-owned-principles`); this umbrella is not itself a Roadmap entry. Support `--template <local-path>` so a project can scaffold from a user-supplied pack, not only the built-in one (git-URL fetch is deferred to `git-url-fetch`; a Nix flake-ref stays out of scope). Sequenced ahead of optional modules (`optional-modules`) because it formalises the pack/manifest abstraction modules slot into.

Background: the original `assets()` in `src/main.rs` hardcoded the mapping from each built-in pack file to its destination path, ownership (reference vs working), and whether it is rendered (substituted) or copied verbatim; one source can fan out to several assets (`AGENTS.md` becomes both the working root file and the `.agents/` reference, both rendered). These steps turn that mapping into data.

Decisions adopted from the resolved open questions:

- **Manifest, dogfooded (OQ-E).** A pack is a directory with a `pack.toml` manifest declaring its assets and variables; the built-in pack ships the same manifest and loads through one path, replacing the hardcoded mapping in `assets()`. Encodes the pack structure as data (Principle 5), one load path with no built-in-versus-external special-casing (Principle 1).
- **Schema (OQ-F).** Each `[[asset]]` is `{ source, dest, ownership = reference|working, render }`; a source may map to several assets. `render = true` applies minimal `{{name}}` substitution with no template engine (Principle 2): `{{principles}}` is a built-in variable computed from the selection, and packs declare other variables (with defaults) that `--var key=value` overrides. A `[[module]]` section is reserved by keeping the parser tolerant of unknown keys (serde ignores them), so these steps carry no dead field and `optional-modules` adds the field when it consumes modules. Behavioural modules (isolation) stay tool flags, not manifest content.
- **Embedding (OQ-G).** The built-in `pack/` is embedded with the `include_dir` crate so built-in and external packs both present as a directory of files under one loader, removing the hand-maintained filename-to-content map (Principle 1); accepted over dependency-free `include_str!` per the user's decision.

Each sub-step is evidence-grounded: validate the approach with a proof-of-concept (build plus tests plus, where relevant, a functional run); on failure fall back to the recorded next-best; if exhausted, raise the impasse.

#### `pack-manifest`: Pack manifest and built-in refactor

Added `pack/pack.toml` declaring the seven built-in assets (`source`, `dest`, `ownership`, `render`), embedded `pack/` with `include_dir`, and added the `manifest` module: `Ownership`, `AssetSpec`, `Manifest`, a resolved `Asset { dest, contents, ownership }`, a `PackSource` (`Embedded`/`Directory`) with a read-by-relative-path interface, `render(template, vars)` (`{{key}}` substitution, unknown placeholders left as-is), and `load(source, vars)` producing the asset set in manifest order. `main::assets()` and its `include_str!` constants are gone; `scaffold` now builds a `{{principles}}` variable from the selection and calls `manifest::load(&manifest::builtin(), &vars)`. `pack::render_agents` was removed (substitution now lives in `manifest::render`); `pack::render_principles` supplies the `{{principles}}` value. Validated by proof-of-concept: scaffold output is byte-identical to the pre-refactor golden (`diff -r` clean across all seven files), all four existing drop tests pass unchanged, plus four new `manifest` tests (render substitution, the built-in asset list, render-only-the-rendered-assets, and a filesystem `Directory` fixture proving the loader is source-agnostic); clippy `-D warnings` clean, 37 tests pass. The `Directory` variant carries `#[allow(dead_code)]` (constructed only in the test build until `external-packs` wires `--template`; `expect` would be unfulfilled in the test build). The fallback (keep the hardcoded `assets()`) was not needed.

#### `external-packs`: External local-path packs and variables

Added `--template <path>` (an external pack directory loaded through the same manifest loader as the built-in) and `--var key=value` (repeatable). `main` builds the `PackSource` once (built-in or `Directory`) and threads it into `scaffold`. The manifest gained a `[[var]]` array (`VarSpec { name, default: Option<String> }`); `manifest::load` now takes the built-in variables and the `--var` overrides, resolves the substitution map (`resolve_vars`), and returns a `LoadError` (`Io`, `UndeclaredVar`, `MissingRequiredVar`, `ReservedVar`, with `Display` and exit 2). Validated by proof-of-concept: 41 tests pass (four new `manifest` variable tests: default-applies/override-wins, missing-required error, undeclared-override error, reserved-name rejection for both declaration and override); the built-in pack still scaffolds byte-identically with no `--template` (`diff -r` clean); and a functional external-pack run confirmed rendered-versus-verbatim assets, ownership, optional defaults, overrides, and that each error path (missing required, undeclared, reserved, malformed `--var`, missing template directory) exits 2 and writes nothing. clippy `-D warnings` clean. The `Directory` variant's earlier `#[allow(dead_code)]` was removed (now constructed by `main`). The fallback (split loaders) was not needed. Interim limitation per OQ-J: `{{principles}}` and selection still come from the built-in set even under `--template`; `pack-owned-principles` removes this.

Decisions adopted from the resolved open questions:

- **Variable schema (OQ-I).** Variables are declared as a `[[var]]` array of tables, each `{ name, default? }` (a `VarSpec { name: String, default: Option<String> }`); a present `default` marks the variable optional, an absent `default` marks it required. Chosen over a `[vars]` name-to-default table (cannot express a required variable) and a string-or-table `[vars]` (untagged parsing complexity): the array of tables encodes required-versus-optional as `Option` rather than a sentinel (Principle 5), matches the `[[asset]]` convention already in the manifest (Principle 1), and needs no template engine (Principle 2).
- **Variable resolution and validation.** Resolve the substitution map from three sources: the built-in `principles` variable, then each declared variable's default, then `--var` overrides. Errors (a `TemplateError` enum with `Display`, exiting 2 like `SelectionError`): a `--var` naming a variable the pack does not declare; a required variable (no default) that no `--var` supplies; and any use of the reserved name `principles` (a pack declaring a `[[var]]` named `principles`, or a `--var principles=...`), mirroring the existing reserved-token guard in `resolve_selection`. Unknown `{{...}}` placeholders in asset bodies stay left as-is, as in `pack-manifest`.
- **Principle source, interim (OQ-J).** Here, `{{principles}}` and principle selection still come from the built-in embedded set even under `--template`; this is a documented interim limitation that `pack-owned-principles` removes. To keep that change localized (no back-tracking), `main` constructs the `PackSource` (built-in or `--template` `Directory`) once and threads it into `scaffold` in this step, so the asset load already flows through the chosen source.

Validate: a fixture pack (a temp directory with a `pack.toml` declaring an optional and a required variable and a templated asset that uses them) scaffolds correctly, honouring ownership, render, and `--var`; the required-variable, undeclared-variable, and reserved-name errors each fire; and the built-in pack still scaffolds byte-identically with no `--template`. Fallback: if a filesystem source cannot share the embedded loader cleanly, split the loaders but keep one manifest schema, and raise it.

#### `pack-owned-principles`: Pack-owned principles

Principles are now a property of the active pack. `PackSource::read` was made public and `main` gained a `pack_principles(source)` helper that parses the pack's `principles.toml` (empty set when the pack ships none; a malformed file is a parse error exiting 2); `main` uses it in place of `pack::default_principles()`, so selection, `--list-principles`, the interactive selector, and the `{{principles}}` value all reflect the active pack. For the built-in pack this reads the same embedded `principles.toml` the drop uses, so the built-in path is unchanged. `pack::default_principles`/`DEFAULT_PRINCIPLES_TOML` became `#[cfg(test)]` (production reads through the source; they remain for the pack-data tests), keeping the production build free of dead code. Validated by proof-of-concept: 44 tests pass (three new: built-in principles read through the source match the helper, an external pack's `principles.toml` drives selection with no built-in leak, and a pack with no `principles.toml` yields an empty set); functional runs confirmed `--list-principles --template` and `--principles all --template` list the pack's principles, a `--template` scaffold renders the pack's principles into `AGENTS.md` (no built-in principle present) and drops the pack's `principles.toml`, and a pack without principles renders `{{principles}}` empty without error; the built-in scaffold is byte-identical to the golden; clippy `-D warnings` clean. The fallback (keep the built-in set for external packs) was not needed. Known minor limitation (out of scope): the interactive save modal's summary text still names the built-in file set even under `--template`.

### `init-vcs`: Initialise a VCS repository (git by default)

`--vcs <git|none>` (default `git`) initialises an empty version-control repository in the output directory. Git is initialised by default, matching cargo; `--vcs none` skips it. This reverses OQ-K's original opt-in `--init` default (the user chose cargo's on-by-default convention); it is reconciled with safe-on-existing (Principle 3) by never nesting or reinitialising (see below), and `--vcs none` preserves the opt-out. Behaviour:

- Default git, `none` to skip: `--vcs` is a clap value-enum (`Vcs { Git, None }`) defaulting to `Git`; the reserved future extension (jj, hg, and so on) slots in as more variants without changing the default.
- Safe on existing (cargo-style): `inside_git_repo` walks up from the output directory (resolving a relative path against the current directory) looking for a `.git` entry; if the directory is already inside a repository the action is skipped, so scaffolding into a subdirectory of an existing repo never nests a new one, and an existing repo is never reinitialised (Principle 3).
- Mechanism: `run_git_init` shells out to `git init <output-dir>` (no libgit2 or Nix dependency, consistent with `git-url-fetch`). If a repository would be initialised but the `git` binary is not found, the run errors (exit 2) rather than silently skipping; `--vcs none` needs no git.
- Write-safety and idempotency: `init_plan` decides the action from a read only (`InitPlan::Init` / `SkipExists` / `None`), so the preview never writes. The repository line appears first in the plan (`init  git repository`, or `skip (exists)  git repository` when already inside a repo) and `git init` runs only when writing is confirmed (`--write` or the selector's Save), before the assets are dropped. The repository is left empty (no initial commit and no `.gitignore`); committing the scaffolded files stays the user's choice.

Validated by proof-of-concept: a unit test asserts `init_plan`'s decision (default git in a fresh directory -> init, `--vcs none` -> none, a directory that is a repository -> skip, a subdirectory of a repository -> skip so nothing nests) without needing a git binary; and functional runs confirmed that a default run into a fresh directory with `--write` creates `.git`, `--vcs none` writes no repository, scaffolding into a subdirectory of an existing repo skips (no nested repo), and a default run with `git` absent from `PATH` errors (exit 2) before any asset is written. 46 tests pass; clippy `-D warnings` clean. The fallback (raise rather than add a git-library dependency) was not needed.

### Workflow hardening (from the round-1 review)

Shared context for the eight steps below (`convergence-accounting`, `workflow-doc-fixes`, `pack-rebuild-tracking`, `triager-independence`, `human-onboarding`, `deliberation-mode`, `human-review-queue`, `ledger-template`); this umbrella is not itself a Roadmap entry. They fold in the outcome of the workflow-hardening review round (round 1, 14 triager-valid findings; see `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md`), four human-queued workflow-interface features, a build-system fix found while implementing `convergence-accounting` (`pack-rebuild-tracking`), and a human workflow-rule decision (`triager-independence`). The correctness fixes (`convergence-accounting`, `workflow-doc-fixes`, `pack-rebuild-tracking`) come before the feature work. All change the canonical pack assets (`pack/AGENTS.md`, the prompts under `pack/prompts/`, the README diagram, and, where noted, `pack/pack.toml` and the manifest test) except `pack-rebuild-tracking`, which is a build-system fix (a `build.rs`); each is a non-trivial change kept small and reviewable, and each carries the evidence-grounding discipline: validate the change (build plus the relevant tests plus, where a document renders, a read-through that the prose, the prompts, the diagram, and this plan agree); on failure fall back to the recorded next-best; if exhausted, raise the impasse. The finding ids `F<n>` reference the combined findings recorded in the review ledger. The `state-schema` block that follows this cluster (immediately after `ledger-template`) is a separate, deferred item (`Q-11`, sequenced after the human-interface work), not one of these eight hardening steps; the hardening cluster ends at `ledger-template`.

### `convergence-accounting`: Fix the convergence accounting (F1, F2, F7, F10)

The core design fix, addressing F1, F2, F7, and F10. The convergence rule's round taxonomy was not a partition, and the "contested" round state the separate contested-rounds cap depended on was never defined or recorded, so that cap could not be computed from the ledger; the cap-boundary wording was inconsistent (F7) and the convergence bullets omitted the total-round cap (F10).

Decision (`Q-1`, decided as approach (b)): drop the separate contested-rounds cap entirely and bound each review loop with a single total-round cap (default five). The per-round outcome is a two-way partition, `clean` or `new-valid`, with no separate round state for a persisting dispute and no cross-round finding ids; escalation fires when the total rounds on an artifact reach the cap, whatever the clean-versus-new-valid mix. Reasoning: judged against Principle 1 (prefer the cleaner long-term architecture) and Principle 5 (make illegal states unrepresentable), the single cap removes the non-partition taxonomy and the under-defined round state instead of adding per-round state and per-finding identity to track them, so there is no unrecordable state and no ambiguous 3-versus-5 boundary to get wrong. The accepted trade-off: the loop loses the "stuck on the same dispute" signal, so a loop that keeps finding new genuine issues and a loop relitigating one finding escalate on the same schedule; the total-round cap still bounds both.

The fix makes the escalation trigger deterministic and computable from the ledger, and states the same rule the same way in every document: `pack/AGENTS.md` (the Convergence and ledger sections), `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md` (the round-counting steps), the ledger round-summary schema (the `clean`/`new-valid` per-round outcome and the running total-round count), and the README diagram's cap-boundary labels (aligning "cap hit" with the prose so F7's off-by-one is gone). This step also owns the matching correction to the plan's own `core-assets` narrative; that narrative's convergence sentence and round-constant count are updated in this plan now (to a single total-round cap and two round constants) so a resuming agent is not misled, matching how F3 was corrected ahead of `workflow-doc-fixes`. Validate by reading the sources against each other and confirming a re-spawned orchestrator could recompute the escalation decision from the ledger alone.

### `workflow-doc-fixes`: Resolve the remaining valid review findings

The remaining triager-valid findings, each a small, self-contained documentation fix. Grouped here because they are mechanical or near-mechanical; split into separate commits if that keeps each reviewable. Ordering note: the F5 and F16 edits touch the convergence and clean-round text that `convergence-accounting` rewrites first, so they apply on top of that rewrite, not before it.

- F3 (done): the plan's `core-assets` narrative previously called the review ledger "a durable scratch file (not committed)", contradicting `pack/AGENTS.md`, `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md`, and `CHANGELOG.md` ("committed beside its plan"). Already corrected in the narrative (it now reads "committed to version control beside its plan"), applied ahead of this step because the plan is the resume anchor and would otherwise mislead a resuming agent; no further edit is needed here. (Interacts with `ledger-template`, which pins the ledger's home.)
- F4 (severity scale): enumerate the severity scale used by `pack/prompts/reviewer.md` and `pack/prompts/triager.md` once, and reconcile the dismissed-high-severity guard's "high" wording with its stated aim of catching a "critical" finding. The decided scale (`Q-7`) is stated in the "Severity scale" paragraph below; apply it here rather than restating it.
- F5 (re-check overturn): specify what happens when the second-triager re-check overturns a dismissed high-severity finding (flip the round outcome, reset the consecutive-clean streak, send it back to be fixed), and state that convergence blocks until the re-check returns, so the re-check is a step in the loop, not an aside.
- F6 (acceptance loop): state explicitly whether the acceptance review runs the convergence loop, and align the README diagram with the answer (the diagram currently draws acceptance as a single pass with no loop, cap, or escalation, while the prose says "as in the other review phases").
- F8 (per-artifact reset): counts are per-artifact but the ledger is per-task; state the rule that resets the consecutive-clean count and the round total when the loop moves to a new artifact or step, so segmentation is specified rather than inferred.
- F9 (resume reset): state that the escalation-resume path resets the round counters, so a total-cap escalation does not immediately re-fire on resume.
- F12 (diagram routing): fix the README diagram's trivial-interrupt edge (`intake -> impl`), which dead-ends when no step is pending; route it to match the prose (the orchestrator folds a trivial request in directly, so it should re-enter wherever the loop paused rather than always into implementation).
- F15 (risk classification): define and record the risk classification that selects one vs two required clean rounds ("risky / high-blast-radius"), so the choice is recorded rather than subjective.
- F16 (zero-findings clean round): make the zero-findings case explicit in the "clean round" definition, which currently reads as "every finding dismissed" and does not obviously cover a round that found nothing.
- F17 (nit): the plan's `core-assets` narrative called them "three round constants"; under the single-total-cap design (`Q-1`, approach (b)) there are only two (the required consecutive clean rounds and the total-round cap of five), the contested-rounds cap having been dropped. This narrative correction is owned by `convergence-accounting`, which rewrites the same convergence sentence, and is already applied in this plan; nothing separate is needed here.

Fixes span `pack/AGENTS.md`, `pack/prompts/reviewer.md`, `pack/prompts/triager.md`, `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md`, `README.md` (the diagram), and this plan's own `core-assets` narrative (F3, F17). Validate by reading each fixed source against the others; the three invalid findings (F11, F13, F14; cosmetic soft-wrap and a faithful caption) are not addressed, per the triager verdicts.

Severity scale (F4, `Q-7`, decided). The reviewer and triager severity scale is a four-level rating: `low`, `medium`, `high`, `critical`. It is an absolute rating, not a relative ranking (validated against CVSS practice; prioritisation like EPSS/KEV is a separate overlay out of scope here). Enumerate it once in `pack/prompts/reviewer.md` and `pack/prompts/triager.md`, and have the dismissed-finding guard trigger on a dismissed `high`-or-above finding, so it still catches a `critical`; correct the guard's wording (which today says only "high-severity") to match (Principles 1 and 5).

### `pack-rebuild-tracking`: Register the embedded pack as a rebuild dependency

To be done before the golden sync-test in `ledger-template` (found while implementing `convergence-accounting`; see `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md`). `include_dir!` in `src/manifest.rs` embeds `pack/` into the binary at compile time, but the macro does NOT register those files as cargo rebuild dependencies, so after a pack file changes a plain `cargo build` can leave the binary embedding a STALE pack. `just scaffold-self` (and the future golden sync-test in `ledger-template`) then regenerate the root `AGENTS.md` and `.agents/` from that stale embedded pack. This actually happened: the bootstrap's committed `.agents/` copies were stale (missing the hardening content) until a forced `cargo clean -p agent-scaffold` + rebuild caught them up.

Fix: add a `build.rs` that emits `cargo:rerun-if-changed=pack` (or the equivalent per-file directives) so any change under `pack/` forces a rebuild and re-embed. This must land before the golden sync-test in `ledger-template` is worth building, since that test would otherwise be fooled by the same staleness, and before anything relies on `scaffold-self` being current.

Evidence-grounding: edit a `pack/` file, run `cargo build` with no manual `cargo clean`, and confirm the change is re-embedded and appears in a fresh scaffold; on failure fall back to the recorded next-best, and if the candidates are exhausted raise the impasse.

### `triager-independence`: The triager is always a separate agent

Decided by the human (`Q-13`): ALWAYS a separate triager, NEVER collapsed. Amend `pack/AGENTS.md`'s role-separation rules so the triager is always a separate agent, independent of BOTH the agent that produced the artifact under review AND the orchestrator, for every review round including trivial ones. The orchestrator drives the loop and owns convergence and cost, so it is biased toward dismissing findings to converge; letting it triage would let that bias decide which findings count.

Changes to `pack/AGENTS.md`:

- Narrow the "Match the ceremony to the stakes: collapse roles for a trivial change" rule so it explicitly does NOT permit collapsing the triager: the triager stays separate even for a trivial or low-risk review round.
- Update the Triager role description (today it states only "The triager must not be the agent that produced the artifact under review") to add the orchestrator-not-triager independence alongside the existing producer-not-triager rule.

Align `pack/prompts/triager.md` and `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md` with the strengthened rule where they name who triages. Evidence-grounding: read the role-separation rule, the collapse-for-trivial rule, and the Triager role description against each other and confirm they agree that the triager is separate from both the producer and the orchestrator for every review round.

Outcome (complete; implemented in `bd483e1`, converged at `bdebe9d`). Narrowed the collapse-for-trivial rule so the triager is the one exception (never merged into the producer or the orchestrator, even for a trivial or low-risk round), tightened the no-sub-agents fallback in both `pack/AGENTS.md` and `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md` to "the other roles" so it no longer collapses the triager, and added the orchestrator-independence reasoning (the orchestrator owns convergence and cost, so it is biased toward dismissing findings) to the Triager role description and both prompts. Reviewed with two independent reviewers (different models) and a separate triager (round 1: three valid coherence findings fixed, two invalid, including a dismissed high with auditable reasoning), then a fresh verification reviewer and a separate triager (round 2: clean, one cosmetic finding dismissed on the F11/F13/F14 precedent). The invariant is now stated consistently across `pack/AGENTS.md` (opening pointer plus the full Triager-role rule), `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md`, and `pack/prompts/triager.md`, each permitting "(or a human)" as the triager, and the generated root `AGENTS.md` and `.agents/` mirrors are in sync. See `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md` for the round records.

### Durability, recovery, isolation, and user prompts (consolidated workflow rules)

Shared context for the four steps below (`file-safety-rules`, `agent-isolation`, `user-prompts-dir`, `compaction-prep`); this umbrella is not itself a Roadmap entry. They fold in a backlog of already-decided workflow rules gathered while running this task (see the ledger's post-189b7bd notes and everything after). The unifying idea across the durability and recovery rules is that git is the workflow's durability and recovery substrate. `findings-files` (above, with the structured-record cluster) came from the same backlog and depends on the commit-before-delete rule defined here. All of these change the canonical pack assets (`pack/AGENTS.md`, the prompts under `pack/prompts/`, and, where noted, `pack/pack.toml`, the manifest asset-list test, and `README.md`); each is a non-trivial change kept small and reviewable and carries the evidence-grounding discipline (validate the change, on failure fall back to the recorded next-best, and if exhausted raise the impasse).

### `file-safety-rules`: Git-durability-and-recovery discipline

Decided (`Q-17`, adopted by the human). Add to `pack/AGENTS.md` an always-on file-safety discipline that works on any harness, whether or not it offers isolation; this is the baseline that `agent-isolation` layers a structural upgrade on top of, not a substitute for it. Git is the durability and recovery substrate: the rules keep every writer's damage to a visible, committed-or-recoverable diff. The discipline:

- Clean-tree-before-writer: commit pending work (especially the ledger) before spawning a writer agent, so a writer's kill or misstep leaves only a visible uncommitted diff and never risks already-decided state.
- Commit-before-delete: commit any workflow-managed file (a findings file, the ledger at task close, any transient artifact) before deleting it, so the deletion is a committed deletion recoverable from git history.
- Format only your own files: implementers must NOT run repo-wide formatters (`just fmt` / `nix fmt`) or `git checkout` / `git restore` on files they do not own; they format only the files they changed and leave incidental reformatting to the orchestrator.
- Orchestrator recovery protocol: on any agent kill or interrupt, the orchestrator inspects `git status` and the diff, reverts stray temp artifacts, discards or completes partial work, and confirms a known-good tree before continuing.
- Validation-in-scratch: destructive validations run in a temp directory or worktree, not the live tree.

Motivating incidents (both in the ledger's post-189b7bd notes): an implementer ran repo-wide `just fmt` then `git checkout` on the ledger it did not own, clobbering uncommitted ledger notes; and an implementer was accidentally killed mid-task, which a clean tree and a recovery protocol make safe. Change `pack/AGENTS.md` (a file-safety / durability section) and the role prompts that need it (`pack/prompts/implementer.md` for the format-only-your-own-files and validation-in-scratch rules; `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md` for clean-tree-before-writer, commit-before-delete, and the recovery protocol). Validate by reading the rules across `pack/AGENTS.md` and the prompts and confirming each writer role and the orchestrator carry the rule that applies to it, stated once and consistently.

Outcome (complete; implemented in `d86ec0f`, converged at `aeea55b`). Added a "File safety and durability (git is the recovery substrate)" section to `pack/AGENTS.md` carrying the five rules (clean-tree-before-writer, commit-before-delete, format-only-your-own-files, validate-in-scratch, orchestrator recovery-on-interrupt), each attributed to the role that performs it, and folded the role-specific rules into `pack/prompts/implementer.md` (format-only, validate-in-scratch) and `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md` (clean-tree, commit-before-delete, recovery, plus a tree-hygiene reformatting clause). The Roles section now classifies the spawned roles (planner and implementer are writers; reviewers and the triager are read-only with respect to the plan and code, writing only their own findings files) and defines "writer agent", which `agent-isolation` reuses for its read-only carve-out. Reviewed over three rounds (two independent reviewers plus a separate triager in round 1: three valid low coherence findings fixed, notably a loose "writer agent" term, an unowned incidental-reformatting duty, and a duplicated commit-before-delete; round 2 fixed one more valid low finding, "read-only" made precise; round 3 clean). The commit-before-delete rule now owns the ledger-at-close deletion (the ledger paragraph's duplicate clause was dropped in both `pack/AGENTS.md` and `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md`). See `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md` for the round records.

### `agent-isolation`: Capability-tiered writer isolation

Decided (`Q-18`, adopted by the human). Two parts: the isolation RULE (a workflow-doc change, always applicable) and the isolation MECHANISM (optional-module integration, heavier and deferred).

- Rule (in `pack/AGENTS.md`): run each WRITER agent in the strongest isolation the harness supports, in preference order: (1) container isolation via agent-box / agent-images if available, preferred because it isolates filesystem, environment, and a security sandbox; (2) worktree isolation if that is what the harness offers (for example claude-code native worktrees); (3) the `file-safety-rules` discipline as the fallback when the harness has no isolation. Read-only agents (reviewers reading, the triager) need no isolation (Principle 2, minimal by default: do not add machinery a read-only agent does not need). Isolation is the structural upgrade over the file-safety baseline: a killed isolated writer cannot touch the main tree, which directly addresses the two incidents in the ledger (the fmt-plus-checkout ledger clobber and the accidental kill) that are the evidence for prioritising it.
- Mechanism (optional-module work under `optional-modules`, which already lists container and worktree isolation as a behavioural module): our own build and adoption order is worktree-first, since worktree isolation is native to claude-code and cheap now, then agent-box / agent-images container integration as the heavier follow-on that adds environment and sandbox isolation. The runtime selection rule in the workflow docs is container-preferred, but for us today it resolves to worktree anyway.

Change `pack/AGENTS.md` (the workflow's isolation rule, referencing `file-safety-rules` as the fallback tier) and `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md` (the orchestrator selects the tier when spawning a writer). Validate by reading the rule and confirming the tier order and the read-only-needs-none carve-out are stated once, and that the fallback tier is exactly the `file-safety-rules` discipline.

Outcome (complete; rule implemented in `032964a`, converged at `ef875bd`). Added a "Writer isolation (capability-tiered)" section to `pack/AGENTS.md`: run each writer in the strongest isolation the harness supports, container (agent-box / agent-images) > worktree > the `file-safety-rules` discipline as the fallback tier, with the read-only agents (reviewers, triager) needing none, and the isolation mechanism framed as an optional module deferred to `optional-modules` (the rule is the always-applicable selection policy and resolves to the file-safety fallback until the module is built). Added the tier selection to `pack/prompts/orchestrator.md`. The rule reuses the writer/read-only classification and the "writer agent" definition from `file-safety-rules` rather than restating them, and the file-safety section defers the "structural upgrade" framing to this section (one source of truth). Reviewed with two independent reviewers and a separate triager (round 1: two valid low de-duplication findings fixed, two invalid; round 2 clean). The isolation MECHANISM (container and worktree integration) remains to be built under `optional-modules`. See `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md` for the round records.

### `user-prompts-dir`: Separate user prompts from role prompts

Decided (`Q-16`, confirmed by the human). Create a `.agents/user-prompts/` directory for human-invoked prompts (the kickoff prompt and the `compaction-prep` prompt); `.agents/prompts/` stays the role prompts (orchestrator, planner, reviewer, triager, implementer). Human prompts and role prompts are different in kind: a human copies and pastes a user prompt to drive the workflow, whereas a role prompt is handed to a spawned agent.

This REVISES `human-onboarding`: the kickoff prompt moves from an inline block in `pack/AGENTS.md` into `.agents/user-prompts/`, and the `pack/AGENTS.md` "Getting started" section becomes a thin trigger that points to the directory. The user prompts stay thin trigger-prompts so no workflow content is duplicated between them and the canonical `pack/AGENTS.md` (Principle 1). Adding pack assets also touches `pack/pack.toml` (new `[[asset]]` entries for the user-prompts directory) and its expected-asset test in `src/` (the built-in asset-list test), and the README layout description of the scaffolded tree. Validate by proof-of-concept: the new assets drop through the existing loader, the manifest asset-list test is updated and passes, and the README layout names the new directory. (The "Getting started" section that points to `.agents/user-prompts/` is validated by `human-onboarding`, which owns that section; this step only creates the directory the section points to.)

Outcome (complete; implemented in `9c1a88d`, converged after review). Created a `.agents/user-prompts/` directory with a thin kickoff prompt (`pack/user-prompts/kickoff.md`): a human copies it, fills in the bracketed task and optional context, and pastes it to start a task; it points the agent to act as the orchestrator, read `AGENTS.md`, and use or start a plan, and deliberately restates no workflow content (Principle 1). Added the `pack/pack.toml` `[[asset]]` entry (source `user-prompts/kickoff.md` -> dest `.agents/user-prompts/kickoff.md`, ownership `reference` so the template is refreshed and cannot drift, since the human copies it out rather than editing in place), extended the built-in asset-list test in `src/manifest.rs`, and named the new directory in the README layout. Reviewed with two independent reviewers (mechanics; content and scope) and a separate triager: the implementation was found mechanically correct and the prompt thin and usable; two valid low plan-bookkeeping findings (reflect the cluster reorder in the Roadmap; move the "Getting started" validation criterion to its owner `human-onboarding`) were folded into this completion update. The compaction-prep and resume user prompts are added by `compaction-prep`, not here. See `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md` for the round record.

### `human-onboarding`: Getting started, for the human

Decided (`Q-9`): approach B plus a thin C slice. Add a "Getting started, for the human" section to the canonical `pack/AGENTS.md` that onboards the human and points them at the editable kickoff prompt they can copy, fill in, and paste to start a task, plus a brief mirror in `README.md` that points to it rather than duplicating it (one source of truth, Principle 1). The section also states the human's ongoing responsibilities, not just the first step: that decisions accumulate in the Open Questions / human-decision queue, and that the human decides on them when the orchestrator pushes them at a checkpoint. Pairs with `deliberation-mode` and `human-review-queue` as the human-interface cluster.

Revised by `user-prompts-dir` (`Q-16`): the kickoff prompt does NOT live inline in `pack/AGENTS.md`. It moves into `.agents/user-prompts/` (the human-invoked prompts directory), and the "Getting started" section becomes a thin trigger that points to that directory rather than carrying the prompt text, so no workflow content is duplicated. Build this step against that revision (a pointer, not an inline block); `user-prompts-dir` owns creating the directory, the manifest entries, and the moved prompt.

Validate by reading the section and confirming a human with no prior context could start a task and knows their standing decision duty; confirm the "Getting started" section points to `.agents/user-prompts/` for the kickoff prompt rather than embedding it, and that the README mirror points to the canonical section and does not restate it.

Outcome (complete; implemented in `27e6171`, converged at `e722a14`). Added a "Getting started, for the human" section near the top of `pack/AGENTS.md`: a newcomer copies the kickoff prompt from `.agents/user-prompts/kickoff.md` to start a task, the section distinguishes the human-invoked prompts from the role prompts, and it states the ongoing decision duty (the orchestrator brings its recommendation when a decision is needed; the decisions collect in the plan's Open Questions queue, which the human reviews as the work proceeds). Added a brief README mirror that points to the canonical section rather than restating it (Principle 1). Reviewed with two independent reviewers and a separate triager (round 1: three valid findings, an "orchestrator"/"checkpoint" jargon gloss, and two forward-references softened; round 2 clean). Because the push-at-checkpoint discipline (`human-review-queue`) and the full human-input contract (`deliberation-mode`) are sequenced later, the decision-duty wording was softened to the interim pull model the pack enforces today, so the section is accurate at this commit; two follow-ups are recorded in `human-review-queue` and `deliberation-mode` to re-strengthen it (the push wording) and restore the "impasse" trigger when that machinery lands. See `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md` for the round records.

### `gate-prompt-clarity`: Clarify the planner's gate prompts and their routing

Decided (`Q-20`, from a human question during dogfooding). `clarifying-questions.md` and `open-questions-gate.md` are the planner's two human-decision gates: an agent runs them, but the human is the decider (the "me"/"I" in them refers to the human, who answers the clarifying questions and chooses among the open-question options). They are distinct from the role prompts an agent both runs and completes (`reviewer.md`, `triager.md`), and distinct from the human-invoked prompts in `.agents/user-prompts/` (`kickoff.md`), which a human pastes. The design intent is recorded at transcript [07-11T10:17] ("answer at the gates: clarifying questions before work starts ... you're the decider there"); a later compaction flattened "human-decision gate" into "agent-facing role prompt", which is the context this step restores.

Keep them as the planner's gate prompts (do not fold into `planner.md`: they are reusable, separately-invocable gate steps, and the plan template's Open Questions section plus `planner.md` carry the same discipline by design, not by accident). Fix two concrete defects:

- Routing wording. Both prompts address the human as "me"/"I" ("ask me", "for me to review and choose from"), which reads as a direct planner-to-human dialogue. In the role-separated flow the planner is a sub-agent whose questions reach the human via the orchestrator. Reword so the audience (the human) and the routing (a sub-agent returns the questions/options to the orchestrator, which surfaces them and relays the human's decisions) are explicit, while staying correct in the single-agent case.
- README label. The `prompts/` layout block calls them "one prompt per workflow role", but they are planner-phase gates, not roles. Correct the label.

Change `pack/prompts/clarifying-questions.md` and `pack/prompts/open-questions-gate.md` (the routing wording) and `README.md` (the layout label). Validate by reading the two prompts and confirming the human is named as the decider and the sub-agent routing is stated, and that the README no longer mislabels the gates as roles.

Outcome (complete; implemented in `41cf5ca`, converged at `593b71d`). Both gate prompts now name the human as the decider and state the sub-agent -> orchestrator -> human routing (a conditional that stays correct in the single-agent case), replacing the ambiguous "ask me"/"for me to review". The README `prompts/` label is now "role prompts and the planner's decision gates" and the two gates are described as agent-asks / human-decides. Kept as separate planner gate prompts (not folded into `planner.md`). Reviewed with two independent reviewers and a separate triager (round 1: three valid low findings, an "Otherwise" reflow, a "surfaces" verb against the style preference, and an orchestrator gate-relay duty the prompts now assert; round 2 clean). The gate-relay duty (S1) was split out to `deliberation-mode`, which already adds the cross-cutting human-input contract across `orchestrator.md` and the gate prompts, so `orchestrator.md` was not edited here (a scope note is recorded in `deliberation-mode`). See `docs/plans/agent-scaffold.ledger.md` for the round records.

### `compaction-prep`: Compaction-prep prompt and checkpoint section

Decided (`Q-15`, confirmed by the human). Add a reusable compaction-prep USER prompt (in `.agents/user-prompts/`, per `user-prompts-dir`) plus a short "Checkpoint / resuming after context loss" section in `pack/AGENTS.md`. This reuses the durability rules from `file-safety-rules` rather than inventing new ones. The procedure:

- Before a compaction: the orchestrator flushes the plan, the ledger, and the Open-Questions queue to current, verifies the plan's resume-anchor (its Status line) is accurate, and commits everything, so state survives the context loss.
- On resume: reconstruct state from `AGENTS.md` plus the plan plus the ledger.

Keep the wording harness-agnostic: name the plan, the ledger, and the durable notes, not a specific harness memory feature, so the procedure works on any harness. Change `pack/AGENTS.md` (the checkpoint / resume section, referencing the existing durability rules) and add the prompt asset under `.agents/user-prompts/` (with the matching `pack/pack.toml` entry and asset-list test update, shared with `user-prompts-dir`). Validate by reading the section and the prompt and confirming a resuming agent with no prior context can reconstruct state from `AGENTS.md`, the plan, and the ledger, and that the pre-compaction checklist flushes and commits all three durable artifacts.

Outcome (complete; implemented in `226ca33`, converged at `16b1260`).
